27#define LOOK_AHEAD_REQUIRED_RECHECKS 	3
28#define LOOK_AHEAD_DEFAULT_DISTANCE 	5
29#define NSKIPADVANCES_THRESHOLD			3
30
31static inline int32 _bt_compare_array_skey(FmgrInfo *orderproc,
32										   Datum tupdatum, bool tupnull,
33										   Datum arrdatum, ScanKey cur);
34static void _bt_binsrch_skiparray_skey(bool cur_elem_trig, ScanDirection dir,
35									   Datum tupdatum, bool tupnull,
36									   BTArrayKeyInfo *array, ScanKey cur,
37									   int32 *set_elem_result);
38static void _bt_skiparray_set_element(Relation rel, ScanKey skey, BTArrayKeyInfo *array,
39									  int32 set_elem_result, Datum tupdatum, bool tupnull);
40static void _bt_skiparray_set_isnull(Relation rel, ScanKey skey, BTArrayKeyInfo *array);
41static void _bt_array_set_low_or_high(Relation rel, ScanKey skey,
42									  BTArrayKeyInfo *array, bool low_not_high);
43static bool _bt_array_decrement(Relation rel, ScanKey skey, BTArrayKeyInfo *array);
44static bool _bt_array_increment(Relation rel, ScanKey skey, BTArrayKeyInfo *array);
45static bool _bt_advance_array_keys_increment(IndexScanDesc scan, ScanDirection dir,
46											 bool *skip_array_set);
47static bool _bt_tuple_before_array_skeys(IndexScanDesc scan, ScanDirection dir,
48										 IndexTuple tuple, TupleDesc tupdesc, int tupnatts,
49										 bool readpagetup, int sktrig, bool *scanBehind);
50static bool _bt_advance_array_keys(IndexScanDesc scan, BTReadPageState *pstate,
51								   IndexTuple tuple, int tupnatts, TupleDesc tupdesc,
52								   int sktrig, bool sktrig_required);

54static bool _bt_verify_keys_with_arraykeys(IndexScanDesc scan);

57								  IndexTuple finaltup);
58static bool _bt_check_compare(IndexScanDesc scan, ScanDirection dir,
59							  IndexTuple tuple, int tupnatts, TupleDesc tupdesc,
60							  bool advancenonrequired, bool forcenonrequired,
61							  bool *continuescan, int *ikey);
62static bool _bt_check_rowcompare(ScanKey header,
63								 IndexTuple tuple, int tupnatts, TupleDesc tupdesc,
64								 ScanDirection dir, bool forcenonrequired, bool *continuescan);
65static void _bt_checkkeys_look_ahead(IndexScanDesc scan, BTReadPageState *pstate,

69
70
71/*
72 * _bt_mkscankey
73 *		Build an insertion scan key that contains comparison data from itup
74 *		as well as comparator routines appropriate to the key datatypes.
75 *
76 *		The result is intended for use with _bt_compare() and _bt_truncate().
77 *		Callers that don't need to fill out the insertion scankey arguments
78 *		(e.g. they use an ad-hoc comparison routine, or only need a scankey
79 *		for _bt_truncate()) can pass a NULL index tuple.  The scankey will
80 *		be initialized as if an "all truncated" pivot tuple was passed
81 *		instead.
82 *
83 *		Note that we may occasionally have to share lock the metapage to
84 *		determine whether or not the keys in the index are expected to be
85 *		unique (i.e. if this is a "heapkeyspace" index).  We assume a
86 *		heapkeyspace index when caller passes a NULL tuple, allowing index
87 *		build callers to avoid accessing the non-existent metapage.  We
88 *		also assume that the index is _not_ allequalimage when a NULL tuple
89 *		is passed; CREATE INDEX callers call _bt_allequalimage() to set the
90 *		field themselves.
91 */
92BTScanInsert
93_bt_mkscankey(Relation rel, IndexTuple itup)
94{
95	BTScanInsert key;
96	ScanKey		skey;
97	TupleDesc	itupdesc;
98	int			indnkeyatts;
99	int16	   *indoption;
100	int			tupnatts;
101	int			i;
102
103	itupdesc = RelationGetDescr(rel);
104	indnkeyatts = IndexRelationGetNumberOfKeyAttributes(rel);
105	indoption = rel->rd_indoption;
106	tupnatts = itup ? BTreeTupleGetNAtts(itup, rel) : 0;
107
108	Assert(tupnatts <= IndexRelationGetNumberOfAttributes(rel));
109
110	/*
111	 * We'll execute search using scan key constructed on key columns.
112	 * Truncated attributes and non-key attributes are omitted from the final
113	 * scan key.
114	 */
115	key = palloc(offsetof(BTScanInsertData, scankeys) +
116				 sizeof(ScanKeyData) * indnkeyatts);
117	if (itup)
118		_bt_metaversion(rel, &key->heapkeyspace, &key->allequalimage);
119	else
120	{
121		/* Utility statement callers can set these fields themselves */
122		key->heapkeyspace = true;
123		key->allequalimage = false;
124	}
125	key->anynullkeys = false;	/* initial assumption */
126	key->nextkey = false;		/* usual case, required by btinsert */
127	key->backward = false;		/* usual case, required by btinsert */
128	key->keysz = Min(indnkeyatts, tupnatts);
129	key->scantid = key->heapkeyspace && itup ?
130		BTreeTupleGetHeapTID(itup) : NULL;
131	skey = key->scankeys;
132	for (i = 0; i < indnkeyatts; i++)
133	{
134		FmgrInfo   *procinfo;
135		Datum		arg;
136		bool		null;
137		int			flags;
138
139		/*
140		 * We can use the cached (default) support procs since no cross-type
141		 * comparison can be needed.
142		 */
143		procinfo = index_getprocinfo(rel, i + 1, BTORDER_PROC);
144
145		/*
146		 * Key arguments built from truncated attributes (or when caller
147		 * provides no tuple) are defensively represented as NULL values. They
148		 * should never be used.
149		 */
150		if (i < tupnatts)
151			arg = index_getattr(itup, i + 1, itupdesc, &null);
152		else
153		{
154			arg = (Datum) 0;
155			null = true;
156		}
157		flags = (null ? SK_ISNULL : 0) | (indoption[i] << SK_BT_INDOPTION_SHIFT);
158		ScanKeyEntryInitializeWithInfo(&skey[i],
159									   flags,
160									   (AttrNumber) (i + 1),
161									   InvalidStrategy,
162									   InvalidOid,
163									   rel->rd_indcollation[i],
164									   procinfo,
165									   arg);
166		/* Record if any key attribute is NULL (or truncated) */
167		if (null)
168			key->anynullkeys = true;
169	}
170
171	/*
172	 * In NULLS NOT DISTINCT mode, we pretend that there are no null keys, so
173	 * that full uniqueness check is done.
174	 */
175	if (rel->rd_index->indnullsnotdistinct)
176		key->anynullkeys = false;
177
178	return key;
179}
180
181/*
182 * free a retracement stack made by _bt_search.
183 */
184void
185_bt_freestack(BTStack stack)
186{
187	BTStack		ostack;
188
189	while (stack != NULL)
190	{
191		ostack = stack;
192		stack = stack->bts_parent;
193		pfree(ostack);
194	}
195}
196
197/*
198 * _bt_compare_array_skey() -- apply array comparison function
199 *
200 * Compares caller's tuple attribute value to a scan key/array element.
201 * Helper function used during binary searches of SK_SEARCHARRAY arrays.
202 *
203 *		This routine returns:
204 *			<0 if tupdatum < arrdatum;
205 *			 0 if tupdatum == arrdatum;
206 *			>0 if tupdatum > arrdatum.
207 *
208 * This is essentially the same interface as _bt_compare: both functions
209 * compare the value that they're searching for to a binary search pivot.
210 * However, unlike _bt_compare, this function's "tuple argument" comes first,
211 * while its "array/scankey argument" comes second.
212*/
213static inline int32
214_bt_compare_array_skey(FmgrInfo *orderproc,
215					   Datum tupdatum, bool tupnull,
216					   Datum arrdatum, ScanKey cur)
217{
218	int32		result = 0;
219
220	Assert(cur->sk_strategy == BTEqualStrategyNumber);
221	Assert(!(cur->sk_flags & (SK_BT_MINVAL | SK_BT_MAXVAL)));
222
223	if (tupnull)				/* NULL tupdatum */
224	{
225		if (cur->sk_flags & SK_ISNULL)
226			result = 0;			/* NULL "=" NULL */
227		else if (cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_NULLS_FIRST)
228			result = -1;		/* NULL "<" NOT_NULL */
229		else
230			result = 1;			/* NULL ">" NOT_NULL */
231	}
232	else if (cur->sk_flags & SK_ISNULL) /* NOT_NULL tupdatum, NULL arrdatum */
233	{
234		if (cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_NULLS_FIRST)
235			result = 1;			/* NOT_NULL ">" NULL */
236		else
237			result = -1;		/* NOT_NULL "<" NULL */
238	}
239	else
240	{
241		/*
242		 * Like _bt_compare, we need to be careful of cross-type comparisons,
243		 * so the left value has to be the value that came from an index tuple
244		 */
245		result = DatumGetInt32(FunctionCall2Coll(orderproc, cur->sk_collation,
246												 tupdatum, arrdatum));
247
248		/*
249		 * We flip the sign by following the obvious rule: flip whenever the
250		 * column is a DESC column.
251		 *
252		 * _bt_compare does it the wrong way around (flip when *ASC*) in order
253		 * to compensate for passing its orderproc arguments backwards.  We
254		 * don't need to play these games because we find it natural to pass
255		 * tupdatum as the left value (and arrdatum as the right value).
256		 */
257		if (cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_DESC)
258			INVERT_COMPARE_RESULT(result);
259	}
260
261	return result;
262}
263
264/*
265 * _bt_binsrch_array_skey() -- Binary search for next matching array key
266 *
267 * Returns an index to the first array element >= caller's tupdatum argument.
268 * This convention is more natural for forwards scan callers, but that can't
269 * really matter to backwards scan callers.  Both callers require handling for
270 * the case where the match we return is < tupdatum, and symmetric handling
271 * for the case where our best match is > tupdatum.
272 *
273 * Also sets *set_elem_result to the result _bt_compare_array_skey returned
274 * when we used it to compare the matching array element to tupdatum/tupnull.
275 *
276 * cur_elem_trig indicates if array advancement was triggered by this array's
277 * scan key, and that the array is for a required scan key.  We can apply this
278 * information to find the next matching array element in the current scan
279 * direction using far fewer comparisons (fewer on average, compared to naive
280 * binary search).  This scheme takes advantage of an important property of
281 * required arrays: required arrays always advance in lockstep with the index
282 * scan's progress through the index's key space.
283 */
284int
285_bt_binsrch_array_skey(FmgrInfo *orderproc,
286					   bool cur_elem_trig, ScanDirection dir,
287					   Datum tupdatum, bool tupnull,
288					   BTArrayKeyInfo *array, ScanKey cur,
289					   int32 *set_elem_result)
290{
291	int			low_elem = 0,
292				mid_elem = -1,
293				high_elem = array->num_elems - 1,
294				result = 0;
295	Datum		arrdatum;
296
297	Assert(cur->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY);
298	Assert(!(cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP));
299	Assert(!(cur->sk_flags & SK_ISNULL));	/* SAOP arrays never have NULLs */
300	Assert(cur->sk_strategy == BTEqualStrategyNumber);
301
302	if (cur_elem_trig)
303	{
304		Assert(!ScanDirectionIsNoMovement(dir));
305		Assert(cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_REQFWD);
306
307		/*
308		 * When the scan key that triggered array advancement is a required
309		 * array scan key, it is now certain that the current array element
310		 * (plus all prior elements relative to the current scan direction)
311		 * cannot possibly be at or ahead of the corresponding tuple value.
312		 * (_bt_checkkeys must have called _bt_tuple_before_array_skeys, which
313		 * makes sure this is true as a condition of advancing the arrays.)
314		 *
315		 * This makes it safe to exclude array elements up to and including
316		 * the former-current array element from our search.
317		 *
318		 * Separately, when array advancement was triggered by a required scan
319		 * key, the array element immediately after the former-current element
320		 * is often either an exact tupdatum match, or a "close by" near-match
321		 * (a near-match tupdatum is one whose key space falls _between_ the
322		 * former-current and new-current array elements).  We'll detect both
323		 * cases via an optimistic comparison of the new search lower bound
324		 * (or new search upper bound in the case of backwards scans).
325		 */
326		if (ScanDirectionIsForward(dir))
327		{
328			low_elem = array->cur_elem + 1; /* old cur_elem exhausted */
329
330			/* Compare prospective new cur_elem (also the new lower bound) */
331			if (high_elem >= low_elem)
332			{
333				arrdatum = array->elem_values[low_elem];
334				result = _bt_compare_array_skey(orderproc, tupdatum, tupnull,
335												arrdatum, cur);
336
337				if (result <= 0)
338				{
339					/* Optimistic comparison optimization worked out */
340					*set_elem_result = result;
341					return low_elem;
342				}
343				mid_elem = low_elem;
344				low_elem++;		/* this cur_elem exhausted, too */
345			}
346
347			if (high_elem < low_elem)
348			{
349				/* Caller needs to perform "beyond end" array advancement */
350				*set_elem_result = 1;
351				return high_elem;
352			}
353		}
354		else
355		{
356			high_elem = array->cur_elem - 1;	/* old cur_elem exhausted */
357
358			/* Compare prospective new cur_elem (also the new upper bound) */
359			if (high_elem >= low_elem)
360			{
361				arrdatum = array->elem_values[high_elem];
362				result = _bt_compare_array_skey(orderproc, tupdatum, tupnull,
363												arrdatum, cur);
364
365				if (result >= 0)
366				{
367					/* Optimistic comparison optimization worked out */
368					*set_elem_result = result;
369					return high_elem;
370				}
371				mid_elem = high_elem;
372				high_elem--;	/* this cur_elem exhausted, too */
373			}
374
375			if (high_elem < low_elem)
376			{
377				/* Caller needs to perform "beyond end" array advancement */
378				*set_elem_result = -1;
379				return low_elem;
380			}
381		}
382	}
383
384	while (high_elem > low_elem)
385	{
386		mid_elem = low_elem + ((high_elem - low_elem) / 2);
387		arrdatum = array->elem_values[mid_elem];
388
389		result = _bt_compare_array_skey(orderproc, tupdatum, tupnull,
390										arrdatum, cur);
391
392		if (result == 0)
393		{
394			/*
395			 * It's safe to quit as soon as we see an equal array element.
396			 * This often saves an extra comparison or two...
397			 */
398			low_elem = mid_elem;
399			break;
400		}
401
402		if (result > 0)
403			low_elem = mid_elem + 1;
404		else
405			high_elem = mid_elem;
406	}
407
408	/*
409	 * ...but our caller also cares about how its searched-for tuple datum
410	 * compares to the low_elem datum.  Must always set *set_elem_result with
411	 * the result of that comparison specifically.
412	 */
413	if (low_elem != mid_elem)
414		result = _bt_compare_array_skey(orderproc, tupdatum, tupnull,
415										array->elem_values[low_elem], cur);
416
417	*set_elem_result = result;
418
419	return low_elem;
420}
421
422/*
423 * _bt_binsrch_skiparray_skey() -- "Binary search" within a skip array
424 *
425 * Does not return an index into the array, since skip arrays don't really
426 * contain elements (they generate their array elements procedurally instead).
427 * Our interface matches that of _bt_binsrch_array_skey in every other way.
428 *
429 * Sets *set_elem_result just like _bt_binsrch_array_skey would with a true
430 * array.  The value 0 indicates that tupdatum/tupnull is within the range of
431 * the skip array.  We return -1 when tupdatum/tupnull is lower that any value
432 * within the range of the array, and 1 when it is higher than every value.
433 * Caller should pass *set_elem_result to _bt_skiparray_set_element to advance
434 * the array.
435 *
436 * cur_elem_trig indicates if array advancement was triggered by this array's
437 * scan key.  We use this to optimize-away comparisons that are known by our
438 * caller to be unnecessary from context, just like _bt_binsrch_array_skey.
439 */
440static void
441_bt_binsrch_skiparray_skey(bool cur_elem_trig, ScanDirection dir,
442						   Datum tupdatum, bool tupnull,
443						   BTArrayKeyInfo *array, ScanKey cur,
444						   int32 *set_elem_result)
445{
446	Assert(cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP);
447	Assert(cur->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY);
448	Assert(cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_REQFWD);
449	Assert(array->num_elems == -1);
450	Assert(!ScanDirectionIsNoMovement(dir));
451
452	if (array->null_elem)
453	{
454		Assert(!array->low_compare && !array->high_compare);
455
456		*set_elem_result = 0;
457		return;
458	}
459
460	if (tupnull)				/* NULL tupdatum */
461	{
462		if (cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_NULLS_FIRST)
463			*set_elem_result = -1;	/* NULL "<" NOT_NULL */
464		else
465			*set_elem_result = 1;	/* NULL ">" NOT_NULL */
466		return;
467	}
468
469	/*
470	 * Array inequalities determine whether tupdatum is within the range of
471	 * caller's skip array
472	 */
473	*set_elem_result = 0;
474	if (ScanDirectionIsForward(dir))
475	{
476		/*
477		 * Evaluate low_compare first (unless cur_elem_trig tells us that it
478		 * cannot possibly fail to be satisfied), then evaluate high_compare
479		 */
480		if (!cur_elem_trig && array->low_compare &&
481			!DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(&array->low_compare->sk_func,
482											array->low_compare->sk_collation,
483											tupdatum,
484											array->low_compare->sk_argument)))
485			*set_elem_result = -1;
486		else if (array->high_compare &&
487				 !DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(&array->high_compare->sk_func,
488												 array->high_compare->sk_collation,
489												 tupdatum,
490												 array->high_compare->sk_argument)))
491			*set_elem_result = 1;
492	}
493	else
494	{
495		/*
496		 * Evaluate high_compare first (unless cur_elem_trig tells us that it
497		 * cannot possibly fail to be satisfied), then evaluate low_compare
498		 */
499		if (!cur_elem_trig && array->high_compare &&
500			!DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(&array->high_compare->sk_func,
501											array->high_compare->sk_collation,
502											tupdatum,
503											array->high_compare->sk_argument)))
504			*set_elem_result = 1;
505		else if (array->low_compare &&
506				 !DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(&array->low_compare->sk_func,
507												 array->low_compare->sk_collation,
508												 tupdatum,
509												 array->low_compare->sk_argument)))
510			*set_elem_result = -1;
511	}
512
513	/*
514	 * Assert that any keys that were assumed to be satisfied already (due to
515	 * caller passing cur_elem_trig=true) really are satisfied as expected
516	 */
517#ifdef USE_ASSERT_CHECKING
518	if (cur_elem_trig)
519	{
520		if (ScanDirectionIsForward(dir) && array->low_compare)
521			Assert(DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(&array->low_compare->sk_func,
522												  array->low_compare->sk_collation,
523												  tupdatum,
524												  array->low_compare->sk_argument)));
525
526		if (ScanDirectionIsBackward(dir) && array->high_compare)
527			Assert(DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(&array->high_compare->sk_func,
528												  array->high_compare->sk_collation,
529												  tupdatum,
530												  array->high_compare->sk_argument)));
531	}
532#endif
533}
534
535/*
536 * _bt_skiparray_set_element() -- Set skip array scan key's sk_argument
537 *
538 * Caller passes set_elem_result returned by _bt_binsrch_skiparray_skey for
539 * caller's tupdatum/tupnull.
540 *
541 * We copy tupdatum/tupnull into skey's sk_argument iff set_elem_result == 0.
542 * Otherwise, we set skey to either the lowest or highest value that's within
543 * the range of caller's skip array (whichever is the best available match to
544 * tupdatum/tupnull that is still within the range of the skip array according
545 * to _bt_binsrch_skiparray_skey/set_elem_result).
546 */
547static void
548_bt_skiparray_set_element(Relation rel, ScanKey skey, BTArrayKeyInfo *array,
549						  int32 set_elem_result, Datum tupdatum, bool tupnull)
550{
551	Assert(skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP);
552	Assert(skey->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY);
553
554	if (set_elem_result)
555	{
556		/* tupdatum/tupnull is out of the range of the skip array */
557		Assert(!array->null_elem);
558
559		_bt_array_set_low_or_high(rel, skey, array, set_elem_result < 0);
560		return;
561	}
562
563	/* Advance skip array to tupdatum (or tupnull) value */
564	if (unlikely(tupnull))
565	{
566		_bt_skiparray_set_isnull(rel, skey, array);
567		return;
568	}
569
570	/* Free memory previously allocated for sk_argument if needed */
571	if (!array->attbyval && skey->sk_argument)
572		pfree(DatumGetPointer(skey->sk_argument));
573
574	/* tupdatum becomes new sk_argument/new current element */
575	skey->sk_flags &= ~(SK_SEARCHNULL | SK_ISNULL |
576						SK_BT_MINVAL | SK_BT_MAXVAL |
577						SK_BT_NEXT | SK_BT_PRIOR);
578	skey->sk_argument = datumCopy(tupdatum, array->attbyval, array->attlen);
579}
580
581/*
582 * _bt_skiparray_set_isnull() -- set skip array scan key to NULL
583 */
584static void
585_bt_skiparray_set_isnull(Relation rel, ScanKey skey, BTArrayKeyInfo *array)
586{
587	Assert(skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP);
588	Assert(skey->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY);
589	Assert(array->null_elem && !array->low_compare && !array->high_compare);
590
591	/* Free memory previously allocated for sk_argument if needed */
592	if (!array->attbyval && skey->sk_argument)
593		pfree(DatumGetPointer(skey->sk_argument));
594
595	/* NULL becomes new sk_argument/new current element */
596	skey->sk_argument = (Datum) 0;
597	skey->sk_flags &= ~(SK_BT_MINVAL | SK_BT_MAXVAL |
598						SK_BT_NEXT | SK_BT_PRIOR);
599	skey->sk_flags |= (SK_SEARCHNULL | SK_ISNULL);
600}
601
602/*
603 * _bt_start_array_keys() -- Initialize array keys at start of a scan
604 *
605 * Set up the cur_elem counters and fill in the first sk_argument value for
606 * each array scankey.
607 */
608void
609_bt_start_array_keys(IndexScanDesc scan, ScanDirection dir)
610{
611	Relation	rel = scan->indexRelation;
612	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
613
614	Assert(so->numArrayKeys);
615	Assert(so->qual_ok);
616
617	for (int i = 0; i < so->numArrayKeys; i++)
618	{
619		BTArrayKeyInfo *array = &so->arrayKeys[i];
620		ScanKey		skey = &so->keyData[array->scan_key];
621
622		Assert(skey->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY);
623
624		_bt_array_set_low_or_high(rel, skey, array,
625								  ScanDirectionIsForward(dir));
626	}
627	so->scanBehind = so->oppositeDirCheck = false;	/* reset */
628}
629
630/*
631 * _bt_array_set_low_or_high() -- Set array scan key to lowest/highest element
632 *
633 * Caller also passes associated scan key, which will have its argument set to
634 * the lowest/highest array value in passing.
635 */
636static void
637_bt_array_set_low_or_high(Relation rel, ScanKey skey, BTArrayKeyInfo *array,
638						  bool low_not_high)
639{
640	Assert(skey->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY);
641
642	if (array->num_elems != -1)
643	{
644		/* set low or high element for SAOP array */
645		int			set_elem = 0;
646
647		Assert(!(skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP));
648
649		if (!low_not_high)
650			set_elem = array->num_elems - 1;
651
652		/*
653		 * Just copy over array datum (only skip arrays require freeing and
654		 * allocating memory for sk_argument)
655		 */
656		array->cur_elem = set_elem;
657		skey->sk_argument = array->elem_values[set_elem];
658
659		return;
660	}
661
662	/* set low or high element for skip array */
663	Assert(skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP);
664	Assert(array->num_elems == -1);
665
666	/* Free memory previously allocated for sk_argument if needed */
667	if (!array->attbyval && skey->sk_argument)
668		pfree(DatumGetPointer(skey->sk_argument));
669
670	/* Reset flags */
671	skey->sk_argument = (Datum) 0;
672	skey->sk_flags &= ~(SK_SEARCHNULL | SK_ISNULL |
673						SK_BT_MINVAL | SK_BT_MAXVAL |
674						SK_BT_NEXT | SK_BT_PRIOR);
675
676	if (array->null_elem &&
677		(low_not_high == ((skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_NULLS_FIRST) != 0)))
678	{
679		/* Requested element (either lowest or highest) has the value NULL */
680		skey->sk_flags |= (SK_SEARCHNULL | SK_ISNULL);
681	}
682	else if (low_not_high)
683	{
684		/* Setting array to lowest element (according to low_compare) */
685		skey->sk_flags |= SK_BT_MINVAL;
686	}
687	else
688	{
689		/* Setting array to highest element (according to high_compare) */
690		skey->sk_flags |= SK_BT_MAXVAL;
691	}
692}
693
694/*
695 * _bt_array_decrement() -- decrement array scan key's sk_argument
696 *
697 * Return value indicates whether caller's array was successfully decremented.
698 * Cannot decrement an array whose current element is already the first one.
699 */
700static bool
701_bt_array_decrement(Relation rel, ScanKey skey, BTArrayKeyInfo *array)
702{
703	bool		uflow = false;
704	Datum		dec_sk_argument;
705
706	Assert(skey->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY);
707	Assert(!(skey->sk_flags & (SK_BT_MAXVAL | SK_BT_NEXT | SK_BT_PRIOR)));
708
709	/* SAOP array? */
710	if (array->num_elems != -1)
711	{
712		Assert(!(skey->sk_flags & (SK_BT_SKIP | SK_BT_MINVAL | SK_BT_MAXVAL)));
713		if (array->cur_elem > 0)
714		{
715			/*
716			 * Just decrement current element, and assign its datum to skey
717			 * (only skip arrays need us to free existing sk_argument memory)
718			 */
719			array->cur_elem--;
720			skey->sk_argument = array->elem_values[array->cur_elem];
721
722			/* Successfully decremented array */
723			return true;
724		}
725
726		/* Cannot decrement to before first array element */
727		return false;
728	}
729
730	/* Nope, this is a skip array */
731	Assert(skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP);
732
733	/*
734	 * The sentinel value that represents the minimum value within the range
735	 * of a skip array (often just -inf) is never decrementable
736	 */
737	if (skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_MINVAL)
738		return false;
739
740	/*
741	 * When the current array element is NULL, and the lowest sorting value in
742	 * the index is also NULL, we cannot decrement before first array element
743	 */
744	if ((skey->sk_flags & SK_ISNULL) && (skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_NULLS_FIRST))
745		return false;
746
747	/*
748	 * Opclasses without skip support "decrement" the scan key's current
749	 * element by setting the PRIOR flag.  The true prior value is determined
750	 * by repositioning to the last index tuple < existing sk_argument/current
751	 * array element.  Note that this works in the usual way when the scan key
752	 * is already marked ISNULL (i.e. when the current element is NULL).
753	 */
754	if (!array->sksup)
755	{
756		/* Successfully "decremented" array */
757		skey->sk_flags |= SK_BT_PRIOR;
758		return true;
759	}
760
761	/*
762	 * Opclasses with skip support directly decrement sk_argument
763	 */
764	if (skey->sk_flags & SK_ISNULL)
765	{
766		Assert(!(skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_NULLS_FIRST));
767
768		/*
769		 * Existing sk_argument/array element is NULL (for an IS NULL qual).
770		 *
771		 * "Decrement" from NULL to the high_elem value provided by opclass
772		 * skip support routine.
773		 */
774		skey->sk_flags &= ~(SK_SEARCHNULL | SK_ISNULL);
775		skey->sk_argument = datumCopy(array->sksup->high_elem,
776									  array->attbyval, array->attlen);
777		return true;
778	}
779
780	/*
781	 * Ask opclass support routine to provide decremented copy of existing
782	 * non-NULL sk_argument
783	 */
784	dec_sk_argument = array->sksup->decrement(rel, skey->sk_argument, &uflow);
785	if (unlikely(uflow))
786	{
787		/* dec_sk_argument has undefined value (so no pfree) */
788		if (array->null_elem && (skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_NULLS_FIRST))
789		{
790			_bt_skiparray_set_isnull(rel, skey, array);
791
792			/* Successfully "decremented" array to NULL */
793			return true;
794		}
795
796		/* Cannot decrement to before first array element */
797		return false;
798	}
799
800	/*
801	 * Successfully decremented sk_argument to a non-NULL value.  Make sure
802	 * that the decremented value is still within the range of the array.
803	 */
804	if (array->low_compare &&
805		!DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(&array->low_compare->sk_func,
806										array->low_compare->sk_collation,
807										dec_sk_argument,
808										array->low_compare->sk_argument)))
809	{
810		/* Keep existing sk_argument after all */
811		if (!array->attbyval)
812			pfree(DatumGetPointer(dec_sk_argument));
813
814		/* Cannot decrement to before first array element */
815		return false;
816	}
817
818	/* Accept value returned by opclass decrement callback */
819	if (!array->attbyval && skey->sk_argument)
820		pfree(DatumGetPointer(skey->sk_argument));
821	skey->sk_argument = dec_sk_argument;
822
823	/* Successfully decremented array */
824	return true;
825}
826
827/*
828 * _bt_array_increment() -- increment array scan key's sk_argument
829 *
830 * Return value indicates whether caller's array was successfully incremented.
831 * Cannot increment an array whose current element is already the final one.
832 */
833static bool
834_bt_array_increment(Relation rel, ScanKey skey, BTArrayKeyInfo *array)
835{
836	bool		oflow = false;
837	Datum		inc_sk_argument;
838
839	Assert(skey->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY);
840	Assert(!(skey->sk_flags & (SK_BT_MINVAL | SK_BT_NEXT | SK_BT_PRIOR)));
841
842	/* SAOP array? */
843	if (array->num_elems != -1)
844	{
845		Assert(!(skey->sk_flags & (SK_BT_SKIP | SK_BT_MINVAL | SK_BT_MAXVAL)));
846		if (array->cur_elem < array->num_elems - 1)
847		{
848			/*
849			 * Just increment current element, and assign its datum to skey
850			 * (only skip arrays need us to free existing sk_argument memory)
851			 */
852			array->cur_elem++;
853			skey->sk_argument = array->elem_values[array->cur_elem];
854
855			/* Successfully incremented array */
856			return true;
857		}
858
859		/* Cannot increment past final array element */
860		return false;
861	}
862
863	/* Nope, this is a skip array */
864	Assert(skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP);
865
866	/*
867	 * The sentinel value that represents the maximum value within the range
868	 * of a skip array (often just +inf) is never incrementable
869	 */
870	if (skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_MAXVAL)
871		return false;
872
873	/*
874	 * When the current array element is NULL, and the highest sorting value
875	 * in the index is also NULL, we cannot increment past the final element
876	 */
877	if ((skey->sk_flags & SK_ISNULL) && !(skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_NULLS_FIRST))
878		return false;
879
880	/*
881	 * Opclasses without skip support "increment" the scan key's current
882	 * element by setting the NEXT flag.  The true next value is determined by
883	 * repositioning to the first index tuple > existing sk_argument/current
884	 * array element.  Note that this works in the usual way when the scan key
885	 * is already marked ISNULL (i.e. when the current element is NULL).
886	 */
887	if (!array->sksup)
888	{
889		/* Successfully "incremented" array */
890		skey->sk_flags |= SK_BT_NEXT;
891		return true;
892	}
893
894	/*
895	 * Opclasses with skip support directly increment sk_argument
896	 */
897	if (skey->sk_flags & SK_ISNULL)
898	{
899		Assert(skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_NULLS_FIRST);
900
901		/*
902		 * Existing sk_argument/array element is NULL (for an IS NULL qual).
903		 *
904		 * "Increment" from NULL to the low_elem value provided by opclass
905		 * skip support routine.
906		 */
907		skey->sk_flags &= ~(SK_SEARCHNULL | SK_ISNULL);
908		skey->sk_argument = datumCopy(array->sksup->low_elem,
909									  array->attbyval, array->attlen);
910		return true;
911	}
912
913	/*
914	 * Ask opclass support routine to provide incremented copy of existing
915	 * non-NULL sk_argument
916	 */
917	inc_sk_argument = array->sksup->increment(rel, skey->sk_argument, &oflow);
918	if (unlikely(oflow))
919	{
920		/* inc_sk_argument has undefined value (so no pfree) */
921		if (array->null_elem && !(skey->sk_flags & SK_BT_NULLS_FIRST))
922		{
923			_bt_skiparray_set_isnull(rel, skey, array);
924
925			/* Successfully "incremented" array to NULL */
926			return true;
927		}
928
929		/* Cannot increment past final array element */
930		return false;
931	}
932
933	/*
934	 * Successfully incremented sk_argument to a non-NULL value.  Make sure
935	 * that the incremented value is still within the range of the array.
936	 */
937	if (array->high_compare &&
938		!DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(&array->high_compare->sk_func,
939										array->high_compare->sk_collation,
940										inc_sk_argument,
941										array->high_compare->sk_argument)))
942	{
943		/* Keep existing sk_argument after all */
944		if (!array->attbyval)
945			pfree(DatumGetPointer(inc_sk_argument));
946
947		/* Cannot increment past final array element */
948		return false;
949	}
950
951	/* Accept value returned by opclass increment callback */
952	if (!array->attbyval && skey->sk_argument)
953		pfree(DatumGetPointer(skey->sk_argument));
954	skey->sk_argument = inc_sk_argument;
955
956	/* Successfully incremented array */
957	return true;
958}
959
960/*
961 * _bt_advance_array_keys_increment() -- Advance to next set of array elements
962 *
963 * Advances the array keys by a single increment in the current scan
964 * direction.  When there are multiple array keys this can roll over from the
965 * lowest order array to higher order arrays.
966 *
967 * Returns true if there is another set of values to consider, false if not.
968 * On true result, the scankeys are initialized with the next set of values.
969 * On false result, the scankeys stay the same, and the array keys are not
970 * advanced (every array remains at its final element for scan direction).
971 */
972static bool
973_bt_advance_array_keys_increment(IndexScanDesc scan, ScanDirection dir,
974								 bool *skip_array_set)
975{
976	Relation	rel = scan->indexRelation;
977	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
978
979	/*
980	 * We must advance the last array key most quickly, since it will
981	 * correspond to the lowest-order index column among the available
982	 * qualifications
983	 */
984	for (int i = so->numArrayKeys - 1; i >= 0; i--)
985	{
986		BTArrayKeyInfo *array = &so->arrayKeys[i];
987		ScanKey		skey = &so->keyData[array->scan_key];
988
989		if (array->num_elems == -1)
990			*skip_array_set = true;
991
992		if (ScanDirectionIsForward(dir))
993		{
994			if (_bt_array_increment(rel, skey, array))
995				return true;
996		}
997		else
998		{
999			if (_bt_array_decrement(rel, skey, array))
1000				return true;
1001		}
1002
1003		/*
1004		 * Couldn't increment (or decrement) array.  Handle array roll over.
1005		 *
1006		 * Start over at the array's lowest sorting value (or its highest
1007		 * value, for backward scans)...
1008		 */
1009		_bt_array_set_low_or_high(rel, skey, array,
1010								  ScanDirectionIsForward(dir));
1011
1012		/* ...then increment (or decrement) next most significant array */
1013	}
1014
1015	/*
1016	 * The array keys are now exhausted.
1017	 *
1018	 * Restore the array keys to the state they were in immediately before we
1019	 * were called.  This ensures that the arrays only ever ratchet in the
1020	 * current scan direction.
1021	 *
1022	 * Without this, scans could overlook matching tuples when the scan
1023	 * direction gets reversed just before btgettuple runs out of items to
1024	 * return, but just after _bt_readpage prepares all the items from the
1025	 * scan's final page in so->currPos.  When we're on the final page it is
1026	 * typical for so->currPos to get invalidated once btgettuple finally
1027	 * returns false, which'll effectively invalidate the scan's array keys.
1028	 * That hasn't happened yet, though -- and in general it may never happen.
1029	 */
1030	_bt_start_array_keys(scan, -dir);
1031
1032	return false;
1033}
1034
1035/*
1036 * _bt_tuple_before_array_skeys() -- too early to advance required arrays?
1037 *
1038 * We always compare the tuple using the current array keys (which we assume
1039 * are already set in so->keyData[]).  readpagetup indicates if tuple is the
1040 * scan's current _bt_readpage-wise tuple.
1041 *
1042 * readpagetup callers must only call here when _bt_check_compare already set
1043 * continuescan=false.  We help these callers deal with _bt_check_compare's
1044 * inability to distinguish between the < and > cases (it uses equality
1045 * operator scan keys, whereas we use 3-way ORDER procs).  These callers pass
1046 * a _bt_check_compare-set sktrig value that indicates which scan key
1047 * triggered the call (!readpagetup callers just pass us sktrig=0 instead).
1048 * This information allows us to avoid wastefully checking earlier scan keys
1049 * that were already deemed to have been satisfied inside _bt_check_compare.
1050 *
1051 * Returns false when caller's tuple is >= the current required equality scan
1052 * keys (or <=, in the case of backwards scans).  This happens to readpagetup
1053 * callers when the scan has reached the point of needing its array keys
1054 * advanced; caller will need to advance required and non-required arrays at
1055 * scan key offsets >= sktrig, plus scan keys < sktrig iff sktrig rolls over.
1056 * (When we return false to readpagetup callers, tuple can only be == current
1057 * required equality scan keys when caller's sktrig indicates that the arrays
1058 * need to be advanced due to an unsatisfied required inequality key trigger.)
1059 *
1060 * Returns true when caller passes a tuple that is < the current set of
1061 * equality keys for the most significant non-equal required scan key/column
1062 * (or > the keys, during backwards scans).  This happens to readpagetup
1063 * callers when tuple is still before the start of matches for the scan's
1064 * required equality strategy scan keys.  (sktrig can't have indicated that an
1065 * inequality strategy scan key wasn't satisfied in _bt_check_compare when we
1066 * return true.  In fact, we automatically return false when passed such an
1067 * inequality sktrig by readpagetup callers -- _bt_check_compare's initial
1068 * continuescan=false doesn't really need to be confirmed here by us.)
1069 *
1070 * !readpagetup callers optionally pass us *scanBehind, which tracks whether
1071 * any missing truncated attributes might have affected array advancement
1072 * (compared to what would happen if it was shown the first non-pivot tuple on
1073 * the page to the right of caller's finaltup/high key tuple instead).  It's
1074 * only possible that we'll set *scanBehind to true when caller passes us a
1075 * pivot tuple (with truncated -inf attributes) that we return false for.
1076 */
1077static bool
1078_bt_tuple_before_array_skeys(IndexScanDesc scan, ScanDirection dir,
1079							 IndexTuple tuple, TupleDesc tupdesc, int tupnatts,
1080							 bool readpagetup, int sktrig, bool *scanBehind)
1081{
1082	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
1083
1084	Assert(so->numArrayKeys);
1085	Assert(so->numberOfKeys);
1086	Assert(sktrig == 0 || readpagetup);
1087	Assert(!readpagetup || scanBehind == NULL);
1088
1089	if (scanBehind)
1090		*scanBehind = false;
1091
1092	for (int ikey = sktrig; ikey < so->numberOfKeys; ikey++)
1093	{
1094		ScanKey		cur = so->keyData + ikey;
1095		Datum		tupdatum;
1096		bool		tupnull;
1097		int32		result;
1098
1099		/* readpagetup calls require one ORDER proc comparison (at most) */
1100		Assert(!readpagetup || ikey == sktrig);
1101
1102		/*
1103		 * Once we reach a non-required scan key, we're completely done.
1104		 *
1105		 * Note: we deliberately don't consider the scan direction here.
1106		 * _bt_advance_array_keys caller requires that we track *scanBehind
1107		 * without concern for scan direction.
1108		 */
1109		if ((cur->sk_flags & (SK_BT_REQFWD | SK_BT_REQBKWD)) == 0)
1110		{
1111			Assert(!readpagetup);
1112			Assert(ikey > sktrig || ikey == 0);
1113			return false;
1114		}
1115
1116		if (cur->sk_attno > tupnatts)
1117		{
1118			Assert(!readpagetup);
1119
1120			/*
1121			 * When we reach a high key's truncated attribute, assume that the
1122			 * tuple attribute's value is >= the scan's equality constraint
1123			 * scan keys (but set *scanBehind to let interested callers know
1124			 * that a truncated attribute might have affected our answer).
1125			 */
1126			if (scanBehind)
1127				*scanBehind = true;
1128
1129			return false;
1130		}
1131
1132		/*
1133		 * Deal with inequality strategy scan keys that _bt_check_compare set
1134		 * continuescan=false for
1135		 */
1136		if (cur->sk_strategy != BTEqualStrategyNumber)
1137		{
1138			/*
1139			 * When _bt_check_compare indicated that a required inequality
1140			 * scan key wasn't satisfied, there's no need to verify anything;
1141			 * caller always calls _bt_advance_array_keys with this sktrig.
1142			 */
1143			if (readpagetup)
1144				return false;
1145
1146			/*
1147			 * Otherwise we can't give up, since we must check all required
1148			 * scan keys (required in either direction) in order to correctly
1149			 * track *scanBehind for caller
1150			 */
1151			continue;
1152		}
1153
1154		tupdatum = index_getattr(tuple, cur->sk_attno, tupdesc, &tupnull);
1155
1156		if (likely(!(cur->sk_flags & (SK_BT_MINVAL | SK_BT_MAXVAL))))
1157		{
1158			/* Scankey has a valid/comparable sk_argument value */
1159			result = _bt_compare_array_skey(&so->orderProcs[ikey],
1160											tupdatum, tupnull,
1161											cur->sk_argument, cur);
1162
1163			if (result == 0)
1164			{
1165				/*
1166				 * Interpret result in a way that takes NEXT/PRIOR into
1167				 * account
1168				 */
1169				if (cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_NEXT)
1170					result = -1;
1171				else if (cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_PRIOR)
1172					result = 1;
1173
1174				Assert(result == 0 || (cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP));
1175			}
1176		}
1177		else
1178		{
1179			BTArrayKeyInfo *array = NULL;
1180
1181			/*
1182			 * Current array element/array = scan key value is a sentinel
1183			 * value that represents the lowest (or highest) possible value
1184			 * that's still within the range of the array.
1185			 *
1186			 * Like _bt_first, we only see MINVAL keys during forwards scans
1187			 * (and similarly only see MAXVAL keys during backwards scans).
1188			 * Even if the scan's direction changes, we'll stop at some higher
1189			 * order key before we can ever reach any MAXVAL (or MINVAL) keys.
1190			 * (However, unlike _bt_first we _can_ get to keys marked either
1191			 * NEXT or PRIOR, regardless of the scan's current direction.)
1192			 */
1193			Assert(ScanDirectionIsForward(dir) ?
1194				   !(cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_MAXVAL) :
1195				   !(cur->sk_flags & SK_BT_MINVAL));
1196
1197			/*
1198			 * There are no valid sk_argument values in MINVAL/MAXVAL keys.
1199			 * Check if tupdatum is within the range of skip array instead.
1200			 */
1201			for (int arrayidx = 0; arrayidx < so->numArrayKeys; arrayidx++)
1202			{
1203				array = &so->arrayKeys[arrayidx];
1204				if (array->scan_key == ikey)
1205					break;
1206			}
1207
1208			_bt_binsrch_skiparray_skey(false, dir, tupdatum, tupnull,
1209									   array, cur, &result);
1210
1211			if (result == 0)
1212			{
1213				/*
1214				 * tupdatum satisfies both low_compare and high_compare, so
1215				 * it's time to advance the array keys.
1216				 *
1217				 * Note: It's possible that the skip array will "advance" from
1218				 * its MINVAL (or MAXVAL) representation to an alternative,
1219				 * logically equivalent representation of the same value: a
1220				 * representation where the = key gets a valid datum in its
1221				 * sk_argument.  This is only possible when low_compare uses
1222				 * the >= strategy (or high_compare uses the <= strategy).
1223				 */
1224				return false;
1225			}
1226		}
1227
1228		/*
1229		 * Does this comparison indicate that caller must _not_ advance the
1230		 * scan's arrays just yet?
1231		 */
1232		if ((ScanDirectionIsForward(dir) && result < 0) ||
1233			(ScanDirectionIsBackward(dir) && result > 0))
1234			return true;
1235
1236		/*
1237		 * Does this comparison indicate that caller should now advance the
1238		 * scan's arrays?  (Must be if we get here during a readpagetup call.)
1239		 */
1240		if (readpagetup || result != 0)
1241		{
1242			Assert(result != 0);
1243			return false;
1244		}
1245
1246		/*
1247		 * Inconclusive -- need to check later scan keys, too.
1248		 *
1249		 * This must be a finaltup precheck, or a call made from an assertion.
1250		 */
1251		Assert(result == 0);
1252	}
1253
1254	Assert(!readpagetup);
1255
1256	return false;
1257}
1258
1259/*
1260 * _bt_start_prim_scan() -- start scheduled primitive index scan?
1261 *
1262 * Returns true if _bt_checkkeys scheduled another primitive index scan, just
1263 * as the last one ended.  Otherwise returns false, indicating that the array
1264 * keys are now fully exhausted.
1265 *
1266 * Only call here during scans with one or more equality type array scan keys,
1267 * after _bt_first or _bt_next return false.
1268 */
1269bool
1270_bt_start_prim_scan(IndexScanDesc scan, ScanDirection dir)
1271{
1272	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
1273
1274	Assert(so->numArrayKeys);
1275
1276	so->scanBehind = so->oppositeDirCheck = false;	/* reset */
1277
1278	/*
1279	 * Array keys are advanced within _bt_checkkeys when the scan reaches the
1280	 * leaf level (more precisely, they're advanced when the scan reaches the
1281	 * end of each distinct set of array elements).  This process avoids
1282	 * repeat access to leaf pages (across multiple primitive index scans) by
1283	 * advancing the scan's array keys when it allows the primitive index scan
1284	 * to find nearby matching tuples (or when it eliminates ranges of array
1285	 * key space that can't possibly be satisfied by any index tuple).
1286	 *
1287	 * _bt_checkkeys sets a simple flag variable to schedule another primitive
1288	 * index scan.  The flag tells us what to do.
1289	 *
1290	 * We cannot rely on _bt_first always reaching _bt_checkkeys.  There are
1291	 * various cases where that won't happen.  For example, if the index is
1292	 * completely empty, then _bt_first won't call _bt_readpage/_bt_checkkeys.
1293	 * We also don't expect a call to _bt_checkkeys during searches for a
1294	 * non-existent value that happens to be lower/higher than any existing
1295	 * value in the index.
1296	 *
1297	 * We don't require special handling for these cases -- we don't need to
1298	 * be explicitly instructed to _not_ perform another primitive index scan.
1299	 * It's up to code under the control of _bt_first to always set the flag
1300	 * when another primitive index scan will be required.
1301	 *
1302	 * This works correctly, even with the tricky cases listed above, which
1303	 * all involve access to leaf pages "near the boundaries of the key space"
1304	 * (whether it's from a leftmost/rightmost page, or an imaginary empty
1305	 * leaf root page).  If _bt_checkkeys cannot be reached by a primitive
1306	 * index scan for one set of array keys, then it also won't be reached for
1307	 * any later set ("later" in terms of the direction that we scan the index
1308	 * and advance the arrays).  The array keys won't have advanced in these
1309	 * cases, but that's the correct behavior (even _bt_advance_array_keys
1310	 * won't always advance the arrays at the point they become "exhausted").
1311	 */
1312	if (so->needPrimScan)
1313	{
1314		/*
1315		 * Flag was set -- must call _bt_first again, which will reset the
1316		 * scan's needPrimScan flag
1317		 */
1318		return true;
1319	}
1320
1321	/* The top-level index scan ran out of tuples in this scan direction */
1322	if (scan->parallel_scan != NULL)
1323		_bt_parallel_done(scan);
1324
1325	return false;
1326}
1327
1328/*
1329 * _bt_advance_array_keys() -- Advance array elements using a tuple
1330 *
1331 * The scan always gets a new qual as a consequence of calling here (except
1332 * when we determine that the top-level scan has run out of matching tuples).
1333 * All later _bt_check_compare calls also use the same new qual that was first
1334 * used here (at least until the next call here advances the keys once again).
1335 * It's convenient to structure _bt_check_compare rechecks of caller's tuple
1336 * (using the new qual) as one the steps of advancing the scan's array keys,
1337 * so this function works as a wrapper around _bt_check_compare.
1338 *
1339 * Like _bt_check_compare, we'll set pstate.continuescan on behalf of the
1340 * caller, and return a boolean indicating if caller's tuple satisfies the
1341 * scan's new qual.  But unlike _bt_check_compare, we set so->needPrimScan
1342 * when we set continuescan=false, indicating if a new primitive index scan
1343 * has been scheduled (otherwise, the top-level scan has run out of tuples in
1344 * the current scan direction).
1345 *
1346 * Caller must use _bt_tuple_before_array_skeys to determine if the current
1347 * place in the scan is >= the current array keys _before_ calling here.
1348 * We're responsible for ensuring that caller's tuple is <= the newly advanced
1349 * required array keys once we return.  We try to find an exact match, but
1350 * failing that we'll advance the array keys to whatever set of array elements
1351 * comes next in the key space for the current scan direction.  Required array
1352 * keys "ratchet forwards" (or backwards).  They can only advance as the scan
1353 * itself advances through the index/key space.
1354 *
1355 * (The rules are the same for backwards scans, except that the operators are
1356 * flipped: just replace the precondition's >= operator with a <=, and the
1357 * postcondition's <= operator with a >=.  In other words, just swap the
1358 * precondition with the postcondition.)
1359 *
1360 * We also deal with "advancing" non-required arrays here (or arrays that are
1361 * treated as non-required for the duration of a _bt_readpage call).  Callers
1362 * whose sktrig scan key is non-required specify sktrig_required=false.  These
1363 * calls are the only exception to the general rule about always advancing the
1364 * required array keys (the scan may not even have a required array).  These
1365 * callers should just pass a NULL pstate (since there is never any question
1366 * of stopping the scan).  No call to _bt_tuple_before_array_skeys is required
1367 * ahead of these calls (it's already clear that any required scan keys must
1368 * be satisfied by caller's tuple).
1369 *
1370 * Note that we deal with non-array required equality strategy scan keys as
1371 * degenerate single element arrays here.  Obviously, they can never really
1372 * advance in the way that real arrays can, but they must still affect how we
1373 * advance real array scan keys (exactly like true array equality scan keys).
1374 * We have to keep around a 3-way ORDER proc for these (using the "=" operator
1375 * won't do), since in general whether the tuple is < or > _any_ unsatisfied
1376 * required equality key influences how the scan's real arrays must advance.
1377 *
1378 * Note also that we may sometimes need to advance the array keys when the
1379 * existing required array keys (and other required equality keys) are already
1380 * an exact match for every corresponding value from caller's tuple.  We must
1381 * do this for inequalities that _bt_check_compare set continuescan=false for.
1382 * They'll advance the array keys here, just like any other scan key that
1383 * _bt_check_compare stops on.  (This can even happen _after_ we advance the
1384 * array keys, in which case we'll advance the array keys a second time.  That
1385 * way _bt_checkkeys caller always has its required arrays advance to the
1386 * maximum possible extent that its tuple will allow.)
1387 */
1388static bool
1389_bt_advance_array_keys(IndexScanDesc scan, BTReadPageState *pstate,
1390					   IndexTuple tuple, int tupnatts, TupleDesc tupdesc,
1391					   int sktrig, bool sktrig_required)
1392{
1393	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
1394	Relation	rel = scan->indexRelation;
1395	ScanDirection dir = so->currPos.dir;
1396	int			arrayidx = 0;
1397	bool		beyond_end_advance = false,
1398				skip_array_advanced = false,
1399				has_required_opposite_direction_only = false,
1400				all_required_satisfied = true,
1401				all_satisfied = true;
1402
1403	Assert(!so->needPrimScan && !so->scanBehind && !so->oppositeDirCheck);
1404	Assert(_bt_verify_keys_with_arraykeys(scan));
1405
1406	if (sktrig_required)
1407	{
1408		/*
1409		 * Precondition array state assertion
1410		 */
1411		Assert(!_bt_tuple_before_array_skeys(scan, dir, tuple, tupdesc,
1412											 tupnatts, false, 0, NULL));
1413
1414		/*
1415		 * Once we return we'll have a new set of required array keys, so
1416		 * reset state used by "look ahead" optimization
1417		 */
1418		pstate->rechecks = 0;
1419		pstate->targetdistance = 0;
1420	}
1421	else if (sktrig < so->numberOfKeys - 1 &&
1422			 !(so->keyData[so->numberOfKeys - 1].sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY))
1423	{
1424		int			least_sign_ikey = so->numberOfKeys - 1;
1425		bool		continuescan;
1426
1427		/*
1428		 * Optimization: perform a precheck of the least significant key
1429		 * during !sktrig_required calls when it isn't already our sktrig
1430		 * (provided the precheck key is not itself an array).
1431		 *
1432		 * When the precheck works out we'll avoid an expensive binary search
1433		 * of sktrig's array (plus any other arrays before least_sign_ikey).
1434		 */
1435		Assert(so->keyData[sktrig].sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY);
1436		if (!_bt_check_compare(scan, dir, tuple, tupnatts, tupdesc, false,
1437							   false, &continuescan,
1438							   &least_sign_ikey))
1439			return false;
1440	}
1441
1442	for (int ikey = 0; ikey < so->numberOfKeys; ikey++)
1443	{
1444		ScanKey		cur = so->keyData + ikey;
1445		BTArrayKeyInfo *array = NULL;
1446		Datum		tupdatum;
1447		bool		required = false,
1448					tupnull;
1449		int32		result;
1450		int			set_elem = 0;
1451
1452		if (cur->sk_strategy == BTEqualStrategyNumber)
1453		{
1454			/* Manage array state */
1455			if (cur->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY)
1456			{
1457				array = &so->arrayKeys[arrayidx++];
1458				Assert(array->scan_key == ikey);
1459			}
1460		}
1461		else
1462		{
1463			/*
1464			 * Are any inequalities required in the opposite direction only
1465			 * present here?
1466			 */
1467			if (((ScanDirectionIsForward(dir) &&
1468				  (cur->sk_flags & (SK_BT_REQBKWD))) ||
1469				 (ScanDirectionIsBackward(dir) &&
1470				  (cur->sk_flags & (SK_BT_REQFWD)))))
1471				has_required_opposite_direction_only = true;
1472		}
1473
1474		/* Optimization: skip over known-satisfied scan keys */
1475		if (ikey < sktrig)
1476			continue;
1477
1478		if (cur->sk_flags & (SK_BT_REQFWD | SK_BT_REQBKWD))
1479		{
1480			required = true;
1481
1482			if (cur->sk_attno > tupnatts)
1483			{
1484				/* Set this just like _bt_tuple_before_array_skeys */
1485				Assert(sktrig < ikey);
1486				so->scanBehind = true;
1487			}
1488		}
1489
1490		/*
1491		 * Handle a required non-array scan key that the initial call to
1492		 * _bt_check_compare indicated triggered array advancement, if any.
1493		 *
1494		 * The non-array scan key's strategy will be <, <=, or = during a
1495		 * forwards scan (or any one of =, >=, or > during a backwards scan).
1496		 * It follows that the corresponding tuple attribute's value must now
1497		 * be either > or >= the scan key value (for backwards scans it must
1498		 * be either < or <= that value).
1499		 *
1500		 * If this is a required equality strategy scan key, this is just an
1501		 * optimization; _bt_tuple_before_array_skeys already confirmed that
1502		 * this scan key places us ahead of caller's tuple.  There's no need
1503		 * to repeat that work now.  (The same underlying principle also gets
1504		 * applied by the cur_elem_trig optimization used to speed up searches
1505		 * for the next array element.)
1506		 *
1507		 * If this is a required inequality strategy scan key, we _must_ rely
1508		 * on _bt_check_compare like this; we aren't capable of directly
1509		 * evaluating required inequality strategy scan keys here, on our own.
1510		 */
1511		if (ikey == sktrig && !array)
1512		{
1513			Assert(sktrig_required && required && all_required_satisfied);
1514
1515			/* Use "beyond end" advancement.  See below for an explanation. */
1516			beyond_end_advance = true;
1517			all_satisfied = all_required_satisfied = false;
1518
1519			continue;
1520		}
1521
1522		/*
1523		 * Nothing more for us to do with an inequality strategy scan key that
1524		 * wasn't the one that _bt_check_compare stopped on, though.
1525		 *
1526		 * Note: if our later call to _bt_check_compare (to recheck caller's
1527		 * tuple) sets continuescan=false due to finding this same inequality
1528		 * unsatisfied (possible when it's required in the scan direction),
1529		 * we'll deal with it via a recursive "second pass" call.
1530		 */
1531		else if (cur->sk_strategy != BTEqualStrategyNumber)
1532			continue;
1533
1534		/*
1535		 * Nothing for us to do with an equality strategy scan key that isn't
1536		 * marked required, either -- unless it's a non-required array
1537		 */
1538		else if (!required && !array)
1539			continue;
1540
1541		/*
1542		 * Here we perform steps for all array scan keys after a required
1543		 * array scan key whose binary search triggered "beyond end of array
1544		 * element" array advancement due to encountering a tuple attribute
1545		 * value > the closest matching array key (or < for backwards scans).
1546		 */
1547		if (beyond_end_advance)
1548		{
1549			if (array)
1550				_bt_array_set_low_or_high(rel, cur, array,
1551										  ScanDirectionIsBackward(dir));
1552
1553			continue;
1554		}
1555
1556		/*
1557		 * Here we perform steps for all array scan keys after a required
1558		 * array scan key whose tuple attribute was < the closest matching
1559		 * array key when we dealt with it (or > for backwards scans).
1560		 *
1561		 * This earlier required array key already puts us ahead of caller's
1562		 * tuple in the key space (for the current scan direction).  We must
1563		 * make sure that subsequent lower-order array keys do not put us too
1564		 * far ahead (ahead of tuples that have yet to be seen by our caller).
1565		 * For example, when a tuple "(a, b) = (42, 5)" advances the array
1566		 * keys on "a" from 40 to 45, we must also set "b" to whatever the
1567		 * first array element for "b" is.  It would be wrong to allow "b" to
1568		 * be set based on the tuple value.
1569		 *
1570		 * Perform the same steps with truncated high key attributes.  You can
1571		 * think of this as a "binary search" for the element closest to the
1572		 * value -inf.  Again, the arrays must never get ahead of the scan.
1573		 */
1574		if (!all_required_satisfied || cur->sk_attno > tupnatts)
1575		{
1576			if (array)
1577				_bt_array_set_low_or_high(rel, cur, array,
1578										  ScanDirectionIsForward(dir));
1579
1580			continue;
1581		}
1582
1583		/*
1584		 * Search in scankey's array for the corresponding tuple attribute
1585		 * value from caller's tuple
1586		 */
1587		tupdatum = index_getattr(tuple, cur->sk_attno, tupdesc, &tupnull);
1588
1589		if (array)
1590		{
1591			bool		cur_elem_trig = (sktrig_required && ikey == sktrig);
1592
1593			/*
1594			 * "Binary search" by checking if tupdatum/tupnull are within the
1595			 * range of the skip array
1596			 */
1597			if (array->num_elems == -1)
1598				_bt_binsrch_skiparray_skey(cur_elem_trig, dir,
1599										   tupdatum, tupnull, array, cur,
1600										   &result);
1601
1602			/*
1603			 * Binary search for the closest match from the SAOP array
1604			 */
1605			else
1606				set_elem = _bt_binsrch_array_skey(&so->orderProcs[ikey],
1607												  cur_elem_trig, dir,
1608												  tupdatum, tupnull, array, cur,
1609												  &result);
1610		}
1611		else
1612		{
1613			Assert(required);
1614
1615			/*
1616			 * This is a required non-array equality strategy scan key, which
1617			 * we'll treat as a degenerate single element array.
1618			 *
1619			 * This scan key's imaginary "array" can't really advance, but it
1620			 * can still roll over like any other array.  (Actually, this is
1621			 * no different to real single value arrays, which never advance
1622			 * without rolling over -- they can never truly advance, either.)
1623			 */
1624			result = _bt_compare_array_skey(&so->orderProcs[ikey],
1625											tupdatum, tupnull,
1626											cur->sk_argument, cur);
1627		}
1628
1629		/*
1630		 * Consider "beyond end of array element" array advancement.
1631		 *
1632		 * When the tuple attribute value is > the closest matching array key
1633		 * (or < in the backwards scan case), we need to ratchet this array
1634		 * forward (backward) by one increment, so that caller's tuple ends up
1635		 * being < final array value instead (or > final array value instead).
1636		 * This process has to work for all of the arrays, not just this one:
1637		 * it must "carry" to higher-order arrays when the set_elem that we
1638		 * just found happens to be the final one for the scan's direction.
1639		 * Incrementing (decrementing) set_elem itself isn't good enough.
1640		 *
1641		 * Our approach is to provisionally use set_elem as if it was an exact
1642		 * match now, then set each later/less significant array to whatever
1643		 * its final element is.  Once outside the loop we'll then "increment
1644		 * this array's set_elem" by calling _bt_advance_array_keys_increment.
1645		 * That way the process rolls over to higher order arrays as needed.
1646		 *
1647		 * Under this scheme any required arrays only ever ratchet forwards
1648		 * (or backwards), and always do so to the maximum possible extent
1649		 * that we can know will be safe without seeing the scan's next tuple.
1650		 * We don't need any special handling for required scan keys that lack
1651		 * a real array to advance, nor for redundant scan keys that couldn't
1652		 * be eliminated by _bt_preprocess_keys.  It won't matter if some of
1653		 * our "true" array scan keys (or even all of them) are non-required.
1654		 */
1655		if (sktrig_required && required &&
1656			((ScanDirectionIsForward(dir) && result > 0) ||
1657			 (ScanDirectionIsBackward(dir) && result < 0)))
1658			beyond_end_advance = true;
1659
1660		Assert(all_required_satisfied && all_satisfied);
1661		if (result != 0)
1662		{
1663			/*
1664			 * Track whether caller's tuple satisfies our new post-advancement
1665			 * qual, for required scan keys, as well as for the entire set of
1666			 * interesting scan keys (all required scan keys plus non-required
1667			 * array scan keys are considered interesting.)
1668			 */
1669			all_satisfied = false;
1670			if (sktrig_required && required)
1671				all_required_satisfied = false;
1672			else
1673			{
1674				/*
1675				 * There's no need to advance the arrays using the best
1676				 * available match for a non-required array.  Give up now.
1677				 * (Though note that sktrig_required calls still have to do
1678				 * all the usual post-advancement steps, including the recheck
1679				 * call to _bt_check_compare.)
1680				 */
1681				break;
1682			}
1683		}
1684
1685		/* Advance array keys, even when we don't have an exact match */
1686		if (array)
1687		{
1688			if (array->num_elems == -1)
1689			{
1690				/* Skip array's new element is tupdatum (or MINVAL/MAXVAL) */
1691				_bt_skiparray_set_element(rel, cur, array, result,
1692										  tupdatum, tupnull);
1693				skip_array_advanced = true;
1694			}
1695			else if (array->cur_elem != set_elem)
1696			{
1697				/* SAOP array's new element is set_elem datum */
1698				array->cur_elem = set_elem;
1699				cur->sk_argument = array->elem_values[set_elem];
1700			}
1701		}
1702	}
1703
1704	/*
1705	 * Advance the array keys incrementally whenever "beyond end of array
1706	 * element" array advancement happens, so that advancement will carry to
1707	 * higher-order arrays (might exhaust all the scan's arrays instead, which
1708	 * ends the top-level scan).
1709	 */
1710	if (beyond_end_advance &&
1711		!_bt_advance_array_keys_increment(scan, dir, &skip_array_advanced))
1712		goto end_toplevel_scan;
1713
1714	Assert(_bt_verify_keys_with_arraykeys(scan));
1715
1716	/*
1717	 * Maintain a page-level count of the number of times the scan's array
1718	 * keys advanced in a way that affected at least one skip array
1719	 */
1720	if (sktrig_required && skip_array_advanced)
1721		pstate->nskipadvances++;
1722
1723	/*
1724	 * Does tuple now satisfy our new qual?  Recheck with _bt_check_compare.
1725	 *
1726	 * Calls triggered by an unsatisfied required scan key, whose tuple now
1727	 * satisfies all required scan keys, but not all nonrequired array keys,
1728	 * will still require a recheck call to _bt_check_compare.  They'll still
1729	 * need its "second pass" handling of required inequality scan keys.
1730	 * (Might have missed a still-unsatisfied required inequality scan key
1731	 * that caller didn't detect as the sktrig scan key during its initial
1732	 * _bt_check_compare call that used the old/original qual.)
1733	 *
1734	 * Calls triggered by an unsatisfied nonrequired array scan key never need
1735	 * "second pass" handling of required inequalities (nor any other handling
1736	 * of any required scan key).  All that matters is whether caller's tuple
1737	 * satisfies the new qual, so it's safe to just skip the _bt_check_compare
1738	 * recheck when we've already determined that it can only return 'false'.
1739	 *
1740	 * Note: In practice most scan keys are marked required by preprocessing,
1741	 * if necessary by generating a preceding skip array.  We nevertheless
1742	 * often handle array keys marked required as if they were nonrequired.
1743	 * This behavior is requested by our _bt_check_compare caller, though only
1744	 * when it is passed "forcenonrequired=true" by _bt_checkkeys.
1745	 */
1746	if ((sktrig_required && all_required_satisfied) ||
1747		(!sktrig_required && all_satisfied))
1748	{
1749		int			nsktrig = sktrig + 1;
1750		bool		continuescan;
1751
1752		Assert(all_required_satisfied);
1753
1754		/* Recheck _bt_check_compare on behalf of caller */
1755		if (_bt_check_compare(scan, dir, tuple, tupnatts, tupdesc, false,
1756							  !sktrig_required, &continuescan,
1757							  &nsktrig) &&
1758			!so->scanBehind)
1759		{
1760			/* This tuple satisfies the new qual */
1761			Assert(all_satisfied && continuescan);
1762
1763			if (pstate)
1764				pstate->continuescan = true;
1765
1766			return true;
1767		}
1768
1769		/*
1770		 * Consider "second pass" handling of required inequalities.
1771		 *
1772		 * It's possible that our _bt_check_compare call indicated that the
1773		 * scan should end due to some unsatisfied inequality that wasn't
1774		 * initially recognized as such by us.  Handle this by calling
1775		 * ourselves recursively, this time indicating that the trigger is the
1776		 * inequality that we missed first time around (and using a set of
1777		 * required array/equality keys that are now exact matches for tuple).
1778		 *
1779		 * We make a strong, general guarantee that every _bt_checkkeys call
1780		 * here will advance the array keys to the maximum possible extent
1781		 * that we can know to be safe based on caller's tuple alone.  If we
1782		 * didn't perform this step, then that guarantee wouldn't quite hold.
1783		 */
1784		if (unlikely(!continuescan))
1785		{
1786			bool		satisfied PG_USED_FOR_ASSERTS_ONLY;
1787
1788			Assert(sktrig_required);
1789			Assert(so->keyData[nsktrig].sk_strategy != BTEqualStrategyNumber);
1790
1791			/*
1792			 * The tuple must use "beyond end" advancement during the
1793			 * recursive call, so we cannot possibly end up back here when
1794			 * recursing.  We'll consume a small, fixed amount of stack space.
1795			 */
1796			Assert(!beyond_end_advance);
1797
1798			/* Advance the array keys a second time using same tuple */
1799			satisfied = _bt_advance_array_keys(scan, pstate, tuple, tupnatts,
1800											   tupdesc, nsktrig, true);
1801
1802			/* This tuple doesn't satisfy the inequality */
1803			Assert(!satisfied);
1804			return false;
1805		}
1806
1807		/*
1808		 * Some non-required scan key (from new qual) still not satisfied.
1809		 *
1810		 * All scan keys required in the current scan direction must still be
1811		 * satisfied, though, so we can trust all_required_satisfied below.
1812		 */
1813	}
1814
1815	/*
1816	 * When we were called just to deal with "advancing" non-required arrays,
1817	 * this is as far as we can go (cannot stop the scan for these callers)
1818	 */
1819	if (!sktrig_required)
1820	{
1821		/* Caller's tuple doesn't match any qual */
1822		return false;
1823	}
1824
1825	/*
1826	 * Postcondition array state assertion (for still-unsatisfied tuples).
1827	 *
1828	 * By here we have established that the scan's required arrays (scan must
1829	 * have at least one required array) advanced, without becoming exhausted.
1830	 *
1831	 * Caller's tuple is now < the newly advanced array keys (or > when this
1832	 * is a backwards scan), except in the case where we only got this far due
1833	 * to an unsatisfied non-required scan key.  Verify that with an assert.
1834	 *
1835	 * Note: we don't just quit at this point when all required scan keys were
1836	 * found to be satisfied because we need to consider edge-cases involving
1837	 * scan keys required in the opposite direction only; those aren't tracked
1838	 * by all_required_satisfied.
1839	 */
1840	Assert(_bt_tuple_before_array_skeys(scan, dir, tuple, tupdesc, tupnatts,
1841										false, 0, NULL) ==
1842		   !all_required_satisfied);
1843
1844	/*
1845	 * We generally permit primitive index scans to continue onto the next
1846	 * sibling page when the page's finaltup satisfies all required scan keys
1847	 * at the point where we're between pages.
1848	 *
1849	 * If caller's tuple is also the page's finaltup, and we see that required
1850	 * scan keys still aren't satisfied, start a new primitive index scan.
1851	 */
1852	if (!all_required_satisfied && pstate->finaltup == tuple)
1853		goto new_prim_scan;
1854
1855	/*
1856	 * Proactively check finaltup (don't wait until finaltup is reached by the
1857	 * scan) when it might well turn out to not be satisfied later on.
1858	 *
1859	 * Note: if so->scanBehind hasn't already been set for finaltup by us,
1860	 * it'll be set during this call to _bt_tuple_before_array_skeys.  Either
1861	 * way, it'll be set correctly (for the whole page) after this point.
1862	 */
1863	if (!all_required_satisfied && pstate->finaltup &&
1864		_bt_tuple_before_array_skeys(scan, dir, pstate->finaltup, tupdesc,
1865									 BTreeTupleGetNAtts(pstate->finaltup, rel),
1866									 false, 0, &so->scanBehind))
1867		goto new_prim_scan;
1868
1869	/*
1870	 * When we encounter a truncated finaltup high key attribute, we're
1871	 * optimistic about the chances of its corresponding required scan key
1872	 * being satisfied when we go on to recheck it against tuples from this
1873	 * page's right sibling leaf page.  We consider truncated attributes to be
1874	 * satisfied by required scan keys, which allows the primitive index scan
1875	 * to continue to the next leaf page.  We must set so->scanBehind to true
1876	 * to remember that the last page's finaltup had "satisfied" required scan
1877	 * keys for one or more truncated attribute values (scan keys required in
1878	 * _either_ scan direction).
1879	 *
1880	 * There is a chance that _bt_readpage (which checks so->scanBehind) will
1881	 * find that even the sibling leaf page's finaltup is < the new array
1882	 * keys.  When that happens, our optimistic policy will have incurred a
1883	 * single extra leaf page access that could have been avoided.
1884	 *
1885	 * A pessimistic policy would give backward scans a gratuitous advantage
1886	 * over forward scans.  We'd punish forward scans for applying more
1887	 * accurate information from the high key, rather than just using the
1888	 * final non-pivot tuple as finaltup, in the style of backward scans.
1889	 * Being pessimistic would also give some scans with non-required arrays a
1890	 * perverse advantage over similar scans that use required arrays instead.
1891	 *
1892	 * This is similar to our scan-level heuristics, below.  They also set
1893	 * scanBehind to speculatively continue the primscan onto the next page.
1894	 */
1895	if (so->scanBehind)
1896	{
1897		/* Truncated high key -- _bt_scanbehind_checkkeys recheck scheduled */
1898	}
1899
1900	/*
1901	 * Handle inequalities marked required in the opposite scan direction.
1902	 * They can also signal that we should start a new primitive index scan.
1903	 *
1904	 * It's possible that the scan is now positioned where "matching" tuples
1905	 * begin, and that caller's tuple satisfies all scan keys required in the
1906	 * current scan direction.  But if caller's tuple still doesn't satisfy
1907	 * other scan keys that are required in the opposite scan direction only
1908	 * (e.g., a required >= strategy scan key when scan direction is forward),
1909	 * it's still possible that there are many leaf pages before the page that
1910	 * _bt_first could skip straight to.  Groveling through all those pages
1911	 * will always give correct answers, but it can be very inefficient.  We
1912	 * must avoid needlessly scanning extra pages.
1913	 *
1914	 * Separately, it's possible that _bt_check_compare set continuescan=false
1915	 * for a scan key that's required in the opposite direction only.  This is
1916	 * a special case, that happens only when _bt_check_compare sees that the
1917	 * inequality encountered a NULL value.  This signals the end of non-NULL
1918	 * values in the current scan direction, which is reason enough to end the
1919	 * (primitive) scan.  If this happens at the start of a large group of
1920	 * NULL values, then we shouldn't expect to be called again until after
1921	 * the scan has already read indefinitely-many leaf pages full of tuples
1922	 * with NULL suffix values.  (_bt_first is expected to skip over the group
1923	 * of NULLs by applying a similar "deduce NOT NULL" rule of its own, which
1924	 * involves consing up an explicit SK_SEARCHNOTNULL key.)
1925	 *
1926	 * Apply a test against finaltup to detect and recover from the problem:
1927	 * if even finaltup doesn't satisfy such an inequality, we just skip by
1928	 * starting a new primitive index scan.  When we skip, we know for sure
1929	 * that all of the tuples on the current page following caller's tuple are
1930	 * also before the _bt_first-wise start of tuples for our new qual.  That
1931	 * at least suggests many more skippable pages beyond the current page.
1932	 * (when so->scanBehind and so->oppositeDirCheck are set, this'll happen
1933	 * when we test the next page's finaltup/high key instead.)
1934	 */
1935	else if (has_required_opposite_direction_only && pstate->finaltup &&
1936			 unlikely(!_bt_oppodir_checkkeys(scan, dir, pstate->finaltup)))
1937		goto new_prim_scan;
1938
1939continue_scan:
1940
1941	/*
1942	 * Stick with the ongoing primitive index scan for now.
1943	 *
1944	 * It's possible that later tuples will also turn out to have values that
1945	 * are still < the now-current array keys (or > the current array keys).
1946	 * Our caller will handle this by performing what amounts to a linear
1947	 * search of the page, implemented by calling _bt_check_compare and then
1948	 * _bt_tuple_before_array_skeys for each tuple.
1949	 *
1950	 * This approach has various advantages over a binary search of the page.
1951	 * Repeated binary searches of the page (one binary search for every array
1952	 * advancement) won't outperform a continuous linear search.  While there
1953	 * are workloads that a naive linear search won't handle well, our caller
1954	 * has a "look ahead" fallback mechanism to deal with that problem.
1955	 */
1956	pstate->continuescan = true;	/* Override _bt_check_compare */
1957	so->needPrimScan = false;	/* _bt_readpage has more tuples to check */
1958
1959	if (so->scanBehind)
1960	{
1961		/*
1962		 * Remember if recheck needs to call _bt_oppodir_checkkeys for next
1963		 * page's finaltup (see above comments about "Handle inequalities
1964		 * marked required in the opposite scan direction" for why).
1965		 */
1966		so->oppositeDirCheck = has_required_opposite_direction_only;
1967
1968		/*
1969		 * skip by setting "look ahead" mechanism's offnum for forwards scans
1970		 * (backwards scans check scanBehind flag directly instead)
1971		 */
1972		if (ScanDirectionIsForward(dir))
1973			pstate->skip = pstate->maxoff + 1;
1974	}
1975
1976	/* Caller's tuple doesn't match the new qual */
1977	return false;
1978
1979new_prim_scan:
1980
1981	Assert(pstate->finaltup);	/* not on rightmost/leftmost page */
1982
1983	/*
1984	 * Looks like another primitive index scan is required.  But consider
1985	 * continuing the current primscan based on scan-level heuristics.
1986	 *
1987	 * Continue the ongoing primitive scan (and schedule a recheck for when
1988	 * the scan arrives on the next sibling leaf page) when it has already
1989	 * read at least one leaf page before the one we're reading now.  This
1990	 * makes primscan scheduling more efficient when scanning subsets of an
1991	 * index with many distinct attribute values matching many array elements.
1992	 * It encourages fewer, larger primitive scans where that makes sense.
1993	 * This will in turn encourage _bt_readpage to apply the pstate.startikey
1994	 * optimization more often.
1995	 *
1996	 * Also continue the ongoing primitive index scan when it is still on the
1997	 * first page if there have been more than NSKIPADVANCES_THRESHOLD calls
1998	 * here that each advanced at least one of the scan's skip arrays
1999	 * (deliberately ignore advancements that only affected SAOP arrays here).
2000	 * A page that cycles through this many skip array elements is quite
2001	 * likely to neighbor similar pages, that we'll also need to read.
2002	 *
2003	 * Note: These heuristics aren't as aggressive as you might think.  We're
2004	 * conservative about allowing a primitive scan to step from the first
2005	 * leaf page it reads to the page's sibling page (we only allow it on
2006	 * first pages whose finaltup strongly suggests that it'll work out, as
2007	 * well as first pages that have a large number of skip array advances).
2008	 * Clearing this first page finaltup hurdle is a strong signal in itself.
2009	 *
2010	 * Note: The NSKIPADVANCES_THRESHOLD heuristic exists only to avoid
2011	 * pathological cases.  Specifically, cases where a skip scan should just
2012	 * behave like a traditional full index scan, but ends up "skipping" again
2013	 * and again, descending to the prior leaf page's direct sibling leaf page
2014	 * each time.  This misbehavior would otherwise be possible during scans
2015	 * that never quite manage to "clear the first page finaltup hurdle".
2016	 */
2017	if (!pstate->firstpage || pstate->nskipadvances > NSKIPADVANCES_THRESHOLD)
2018	{
2019		/* Schedule a recheck once on the next (or previous) page */
2020		so->scanBehind = true;
2021
2022		/* Continue the current primitive scan after all */
2023		goto continue_scan;
2024	}
2025
2026	/*
2027	 * End this primitive index scan, but schedule another.
2028	 *
2029	 * Note: We make a soft assumption that the current scan direction will
2030	 * also be used within _bt_next, when it is asked to step off this page.
2031	 * It is up to _bt_next to cancel this scheduled primitive index scan
2032	 * whenever it steps to a page in the direction opposite currPos.dir.
2033	 */
2034	pstate->continuescan = false;	/* Tell _bt_readpage we're done... */
2035	so->needPrimScan = true;	/* ...but call _bt_first again */
2036
2037	if (scan->parallel_scan)
2038		_bt_parallel_primscan_schedule(scan, so->currPos.currPage);
2039
2040	/* Caller's tuple doesn't match the new qual */
2041	return false;
2042
2043end_toplevel_scan:
2044
2045	/*
2046	 * End the current primitive index scan, but don't schedule another.
2047	 *
2048	 * This ends the entire top-level scan in the current scan direction.
2049	 *
2050	 * Note: The scan's arrays (including any non-required arrays) are now in
2051	 * their final positions for the current scan direction.  If the scan
2052	 * direction happens to change, then the arrays will already be in their
2053	 * first positions for what will then be the current scan direction.
2054	 */
2055	pstate->continuescan = false;	/* Tell _bt_readpage we're done... */
2056	so->needPrimScan = false;	/* ...and don't call _bt_first again */
2057
2058	/* Caller's tuple doesn't match any qual */
2059	return false;
2060}
2061
2062#ifdef USE_ASSERT_CHECKING
2063/*
2064 * Verify that the scan's "so->keyData[]" scan keys are in agreement with
2065 * its array key state
2066 */
2067static bool
2068_bt_verify_keys_with_arraykeys(IndexScanDesc scan)
2069{
2070	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
