2071	int			last_sk_attno = InvalidAttrNumber,
2072				arrayidx = 0;
2073	bool		nonrequiredseen = false;
2074
2075	if (!so->qual_ok)
2076		return false;
2077
2078	for (int ikey = 0; ikey < so->numberOfKeys; ikey++)
2079	{
2080		ScanKey		cur = so->keyData + ikey;
2081		BTArrayKeyInfo *array;
2082
2083		if (cur->sk_strategy != BTEqualStrategyNumber ||
2084			!(cur->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY))
2085			continue;
2086
2087		array = &so->arrayKeys[arrayidx++];
2088		if (array->scan_key != ikey)
2089			return false;
2090
2091		if (array->num_elems == 0 || array->num_elems < -1)
2092			return false;
2093
2094		if (array->num_elems != -1 &&
2095			cur->sk_argument != array->elem_values[array->cur_elem])
2096			return false;
2097		if (cur->sk_flags & (SK_BT_REQFWD | SK_BT_REQBKWD))
2098		{
2099			if (last_sk_attno > cur->sk_attno)
2100				return false;
2101			if (nonrequiredseen)
2102				return false;
2103		}
2104		else
2105			nonrequiredseen = true;
2106
2107		last_sk_attno = cur->sk_attno;
2108	}
2109
2110	if (arrayidx != so->numArrayKeys)
2111		return false;
2112
2113	return true;
2114}
2115#endif
2116
2117/*
2118 * Test whether an indextuple satisfies all the scankey conditions.
2119 *
2120 * Return true if so, false if not.  If the tuple fails to pass the qual,
2121 * we also determine whether there's any need to continue the scan beyond
2122 * this tuple, and set pstate.continuescan accordingly.  See comments for
2123 * _bt_preprocess_keys() about how this is done.
2124 *
2125 * Forward scan callers can pass a high key tuple in the hopes of having
2126 * us set *continuescan to false, and avoiding an unnecessary visit to
2127 * the page to the right.
2128 *
2129 * Advances the scan's array keys when necessary for arrayKeys=true callers.
2130 * Scans without any array keys must always pass arrayKeys=false.
2131 *
2132 * Also stops and starts primitive index scans for arrayKeys=true callers.
2133 * Scans with array keys are required to set up page state that helps us with
2134 * this.  The page's finaltup tuple (the page high key for a forward scan, or
2135 * the page's first non-pivot tuple for a backward scan) must be set in
2136 * pstate.finaltup ahead of the first call here for the page.  Set this to
2137 * NULL for rightmost page (or the leftmost page for backwards scans).
2138 *
2139 * scan: index scan descriptor (containing a search-type scankey)
2140 * pstate: page level input and output parameters
2141 * arrayKeys: should we advance the scan's array keys if necessary?
2142 * tuple: index tuple to test
2143 * tupnatts: number of attributes in tupnatts (high key may be truncated)
2144 */
2145bool
2146_bt_checkkeys(IndexScanDesc scan, BTReadPageState *pstate, bool arrayKeys,
2147			  IndexTuple tuple, int tupnatts)
2148{
2149	TupleDesc	tupdesc = RelationGetDescr(scan->indexRelation);
2150	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
2151	ScanDirection dir = so->currPos.dir;
2152	int			ikey = pstate->startikey;
2153	bool		res;
2154
2155	Assert(BTreeTupleGetNAtts(tuple, scan->indexRelation) == tupnatts);
2156	Assert(!so->needPrimScan && !so->scanBehind && !so->oppositeDirCheck);
2157	Assert(arrayKeys || so->numArrayKeys == 0);
2158
2159	res = _bt_check_compare(scan, dir, tuple, tupnatts, tupdesc, arrayKeys,
2160							pstate->forcenonrequired, &pstate->continuescan,
2161							&ikey);
2162
2163	/*
2164	 * If _bt_check_compare relied on the pstate.startikey optimization, call
2165	 * again (in assert-enabled builds) to verify it didn't affect our answer.
2166	 *
2167	 * Note: we can't do this when !pstate.forcenonrequired, since any arrays
2168	 * before pstate.startikey won't have advanced on this page at all.
2169	 */
2170	Assert(!pstate->forcenonrequired || arrayKeys);
2171#ifdef USE_ASSERT_CHECKING
2172	if (pstate->startikey > 0 && !pstate->forcenonrequired)
2173	{
2174		bool		dres,
2175					dcontinuescan;
2176		int			dikey = 0;
2177
2178		/* Pass arrayKeys=false to avoid array side-effects */
2179		dres = _bt_check_compare(scan, dir, tuple, tupnatts, tupdesc, false,
2180								 pstate->forcenonrequired, &dcontinuescan,
2181								 &dikey);
2182		Assert(res == dres);
2183		Assert(pstate->continuescan == dcontinuescan);
2184
2185		/*
2186		 * Should also get the same ikey result.  We need a slightly weaker
2187		 * assertion during arrayKeys calls, since they might be using an
2188		 * array that couldn't be marked required during preprocessing.
2189		 */
2190		Assert(arrayKeys || ikey == dikey);
2191		Assert(ikey <= dikey);
2192	}
2193#endif
2194
2195	/*
2196	 * Only one _bt_check_compare call is required in the common case where
2197	 * there are no equality strategy array scan keys.  Otherwise we can only
2198	 * accept _bt_check_compare's answer unreservedly when it didn't set
2199	 * pstate.continuescan=false.
2200	 */
2201	if (!arrayKeys || pstate->continuescan)
2202		return res;
2203
2204	/*
2205	 * _bt_check_compare call set continuescan=false in the presence of
2206	 * equality type array keys.  This could mean that the tuple is just past
2207	 * the end of matches for the current array keys.
2208	 *
2209	 * It's also possible that the scan is still _before_ the _start_ of
2210	 * tuples matching the current set of array keys.  Check for that first.
2211	 */
2212	Assert(!pstate->forcenonrequired);
2213	if (_bt_tuple_before_array_skeys(scan, dir, tuple, tupdesc, tupnatts, true,
2214									 ikey, NULL))
2215	{
2216		/* Override _bt_check_compare, continue primitive scan */
2217		pstate->continuescan = true;
2218
2219		/*
2220		 * We will end up here repeatedly given a group of tuples > the
2221		 * previous array keys and < the now-current keys (for a backwards
2222		 * scan it's just the same, though the operators swap positions).
2223		 *
2224		 * We must avoid allowing this linear search process to scan very many
2225		 * tuples from well before the start of tuples matching the current
2226		 * array keys (or from well before the point where we'll once again
2227		 * have to advance the scan's array keys).
2228		 *
2229		 * We keep the overhead under control by speculatively "looking ahead"
2230		 * to later still-unscanned items from this same leaf page.  We'll
2231		 * only attempt this once the number of tuples that the linear search
2232		 * process has examined starts to get out of hand.
2233		 */
2234		pstate->rechecks++;
2235		if (pstate->rechecks >= LOOK_AHEAD_REQUIRED_RECHECKS)
2236		{
2237			/* See if we should skip ahead within the current leaf page */
2238			_bt_checkkeys_look_ahead(scan, pstate, tupnatts, tupdesc);
2239
2240			/*
2241			 * Might have set pstate.skip to a later page offset.  When that
2242			 * happens then _bt_readpage caller will inexpensively skip ahead
2243			 * to a later tuple from the same page (the one just after the
2244			 * tuple we successfully "looked ahead" to).
2245			 */
2246		}
2247
2248		/* This indextuple doesn't match the current qual, in any case */
2249		return false;
2250	}
2251
2252	/*
2253	 * Caller's tuple is >= the current set of array keys and other equality
2254	 * constraint scan keys (or <= if this is a backwards scan).  It's now
2255	 * clear that we _must_ advance any required array keys in lockstep with
2256	 * the scan.
2257	 */
2258	return _bt_advance_array_keys(scan, pstate, tuple, tupnatts, tupdesc,
2259								  ikey, true);
2260}
2261
2262/*
2263 * Test whether caller's finaltup tuple is still before the start of matches
2264 * for the current array keys.
2265 *
2266 * Called at the start of reading a page during a scan with array keys, though
2267 * only when the so->scanBehind flag was set on the scan's prior page.
2268 *
2269 * Returns false if the tuple is still before the start of matches.  When that
2270 * happens, caller should cut its losses and start a new primitive index scan.
2271 * Otherwise returns true.
2272 */
2273bool
2274_bt_scanbehind_checkkeys(IndexScanDesc scan, ScanDirection dir,
2275						 IndexTuple finaltup)
2276{
2277	Relation	rel = scan->indexRelation;
2278	TupleDesc	tupdesc = RelationGetDescr(rel);
2279	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
2280	int			nfinaltupatts = BTreeTupleGetNAtts(finaltup, rel);
2281	bool		scanBehind;
2282
2283	Assert(so->numArrayKeys);
2284
2285	if (_bt_tuple_before_array_skeys(scan, dir, finaltup, tupdesc,
2286									 nfinaltupatts, false, 0, &scanBehind))
2287		return false;
2288
2289	/*
2290	 * If scanBehind was set, all of the untruncated attribute values from
2291	 * finaltup that correspond to an array match the array's current element,
2292	 * but there are other keys associated with truncated suffix attributes.
2293	 * Array advancement must have incremented the scan's arrays on the
2294	 * previous page, resulting in a set of array keys that happen to be an
2295	 * exact match for the current page high key's untruncated prefix values.
2296	 *
2297	 * This page definitely doesn't contain tuples that the scan will need to
2298	 * return.  The next page may or may not contain relevant tuples.  Handle
2299	 * this by cutting our losses and starting a new primscan.
2300	 */
2301	if (scanBehind)
2302		return false;
2303
2304	if (!so->oppositeDirCheck)
2305		return true;
2306
2307	return _bt_oppodir_checkkeys(scan, dir, finaltup);
2308}
2309
2310/*
2311 * Test whether an indextuple fails to satisfy an inequality required in the
2312 * opposite direction only.
2313 *
2314 * Caller's finaltup tuple is the page high key (for forwards scans), or the
2315 * first non-pivot tuple (for backwards scans).  Called during scans with
2316 * required array keys and required opposite-direction inequalities.
2317 *
2318 * Returns false if an inequality scan key required in the opposite direction
2319 * only isn't satisfied (and any earlier required scan keys are satisfied).
2320 * Otherwise returns true.
2321 *
2322 * An unsatisfied inequality required in the opposite direction only might
2323 * well enable skipping over many leaf pages, provided another _bt_first call
2324 * takes place.  This type of unsatisfied inequality won't usually cause
2325 * _bt_checkkeys to stop the scan to consider array advancement/starting a new
2326 * primitive index scan.
2327 */
2328static bool
2329_bt_oppodir_checkkeys(IndexScanDesc scan, ScanDirection dir,
2330					  IndexTuple finaltup)
2331{
2332	Relation	rel = scan->indexRelation;
2333	TupleDesc	tupdesc = RelationGetDescr(rel);
2334	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
2335	int			nfinaltupatts = BTreeTupleGetNAtts(finaltup, rel);
2336	bool		continuescan;
2337	ScanDirection flipped = -dir;
2338	int			ikey = 0;
2339
2340	Assert(so->numArrayKeys);
2341
2342	_bt_check_compare(scan, flipped, finaltup, nfinaltupatts, tupdesc, false,
2343					  false, &continuescan,
2344					  &ikey);
2345
2346	if (!continuescan && so->keyData[ikey].sk_strategy != BTEqualStrategyNumber)
2347		return false;
2348
2349	return true;
2350}
2351
2352/*
2353 * Determines an offset to the first scan key (an so->keyData[]-wise offset)
2354 * that is _not_ guaranteed to be satisfied by every tuple from pstate.page,
2355 * which is set in pstate.startikey for _bt_checkkeys calls for the page.
2356 * This allows caller to save cycles on comparisons of a prefix of keys while
2357 * reading pstate.page.
2358 *
2359 * Also determines if later calls to _bt_checkkeys (for pstate.page) should be
2360 * forced to treat all required scan keys >= pstate.startikey as nonrequired
2361 * (that is, if they're to be treated as if any SK_BT_REQFWD/SK_BT_REQBKWD
2362 * markings that were set by preprocessing were not set at all, for the
2363 * duration of _bt_checkkeys calls prior to the call for pstate.finaltup).
2364 * This is indicated to caller by setting pstate.forcenonrequired.
2365 *
2366 * Call here at the start of reading a leaf page beyond the first one for the
2367 * primitive index scan.  We consider all non-pivot tuples, so it doesn't make
2368 * sense to call here when only a subset of those tuples can ever be read.
2369 * This is also a good idea on performance grounds; not calling here when on
2370 * the first page (first for the current primitive scan) avoids wasting cycles
2371 * during selective point queries.  They typically don't stand to gain as much
2372 * when we can set pstate.startikey, and are likely to notice the overhead of
2373 * calling here.  (Also, allowing pstate.forcenonrequired to be set on a
2374 * primscan's first page would mislead _bt_advance_array_keys, which expects
2375 * pstate.nskipadvances to be representative of every first page's key space.)
2376 *
2377 * Caller must call _bt_start_array_keys and reset startikey/forcenonrequired
2378 * ahead of the finaltup _bt_checkkeys call when we set forcenonrequired=true.
2379 * This will give _bt_checkkeys the opportunity to call _bt_advance_array_keys
2380 * with sktrig_required=true, restoring the invariant that the scan's required
2381 * arrays always track the scan's progress through the index's key space.
2382 * Caller won't need to do this on the rightmost/leftmost page in the index
2383 * (where pstate.finaltup isn't ever set), since forcenonrequired will never
2384 * be set here in the first place.
2385 */
2386void
2387_bt_set_startikey(IndexScanDesc scan, BTReadPageState *pstate)
2388{
2389	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
2390	Relation	rel = scan->indexRelation;
2391	TupleDesc	tupdesc = RelationGetDescr(rel);
2392	ItemId		iid;
2393	IndexTuple	firsttup,
2394				lasttup;
2395	int			startikey = 0,
2396				arrayidx = 0,
2397				firstchangingattnum;
2398	bool		start_past_saop_eq = false;
2399
2400	Assert(!so->scanBehind);
2401	Assert(pstate->minoff < pstate->maxoff);
2402	Assert(!pstate->firstpage);
2403	Assert(pstate->startikey == 0);
2404	Assert(!so->numArrayKeys || pstate->finaltup ||
2405		   P_RIGHTMOST(BTPageGetOpaque(pstate->page)) ||
2406		   P_LEFTMOST(BTPageGetOpaque(pstate->page)));
2407
2408	if (so->numberOfKeys == 0)
2409		return;
2410
2411	/* minoff is an offset to the lowest non-pivot tuple on the page */
2412	iid = PageGetItemId(pstate->page, pstate->minoff);
2413	firsttup = (IndexTuple) PageGetItem(pstate->page, iid);
2414
2415	/* maxoff is an offset to the highest non-pivot tuple on the page */
2416	iid = PageGetItemId(pstate->page, pstate->maxoff);
2417	lasttup = (IndexTuple) PageGetItem(pstate->page, iid);
2418
2419	/* Determine the first attribute whose values change on caller's page */
2420	firstchangingattnum = _bt_keep_natts_fast(rel, firsttup, lasttup);
2421
2422	for (; startikey < so->numberOfKeys; startikey++)
2423	{
2424		ScanKey		key = so->keyData + startikey;
2425		BTArrayKeyInfo *array;
2426		Datum		firstdatum,
2427					lastdatum;
2428		bool		firstnull,
2429					lastnull;
2430		int32		result;
2431
2432		/*
2433		 * Determine if it's safe to set pstate.startikey to an offset to a
2434		 * key that comes after this key, by examining this key
2435		 */
2436		if (!(key->sk_flags & (SK_BT_REQFWD | SK_BT_REQBKWD)))
2437		{
2438			/* Scan key isn't marked required (corner case) */
2439			break;				/* unsafe */
2440		}
2441		if (key->sk_flags & SK_ROW_HEADER)
2442		{
2443			/* RowCompare inequalities currently aren't supported */
2444			break;				/* "unsafe" */
2445		}
2446		if (key->sk_strategy != BTEqualStrategyNumber)
2447		{
2448			/*
2449			 * Scalar inequality key.
2450			 *
2451			 * It's definitely safe for _bt_checkkeys to avoid assessing this
2452			 * inequality when the page's first and last non-pivot tuples both
2453			 * satisfy the inequality (since the same must also be true of all
2454			 * the tuples in between these two).
2455			 *
2456			 * Unlike the "=" case, it doesn't matter if this attribute has
2457			 * more than one distinct value (though it _is_ necessary for any
2458			 * and all _prior_ attributes to contain no more than one distinct
2459			 * value amongst all of the tuples from pstate.page).
2460			 */
2461			if (key->sk_attno > firstchangingattnum)	/* >, not >= */
2462				break;			/* unsafe, preceding attr has multiple
2463								 * distinct values */
2464
2465			firstdatum = index_getattr(firsttup, key->sk_attno, tupdesc, &firstnull);
2466			lastdatum = index_getattr(lasttup, key->sk_attno, tupdesc, &lastnull);
2467
2468			if (key->sk_flags & SK_ISNULL)
2469			{
2470				/* IS NOT NULL key */
2471				Assert(key->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHNOTNULL);
2472
2473				if (firstnull || lastnull)
2474					break;		/* unsafe */
2475
2476				/* Safe, IS NOT NULL key satisfied by every tuple */
2477				continue;
2478			}
2479
2480			/* Test firsttup */
2481			if (firstnull ||
2482				!DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(&key->sk_func,
2483												key->sk_collation, firstdatum,
2484												key->sk_argument)))
2485				break;			/* unsafe */
2486
2487			/* Test lasttup */
2488			if (lastnull ||
2489				!DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(&key->sk_func,
2490												key->sk_collation, lastdatum,
2491												key->sk_argument)))
2492				break;			/* unsafe */
2493
2494			/* Safe, scalar inequality satisfied by every tuple */
2495			continue;
2496		}
2497
2498		/* Some = key (could be a scalar = key, could be an array = key) */
2499		Assert(key->sk_strategy == BTEqualStrategyNumber);
2500
2501		if (!(key->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY))
2502		{
2503			/*
2504			 * Scalar = key (possibly an IS NULL key).
2505			 *
2506			 * It is unsafe to set pstate.startikey to an ikey beyond this
2507			 * key, unless the = key is satisfied by every possible tuple on
2508			 * the page (possible only when attribute has just one distinct
2509			 * value among all tuples on the page).
2510			 */
2511			if (key->sk_attno >= firstchangingattnum)
2512				break;			/* unsafe, multiple distinct attr values */
2513
2514			firstdatum = index_getattr(firsttup, key->sk_attno, tupdesc,
2515									   &firstnull);
2516			if (key->sk_flags & SK_ISNULL)
2517			{
2518				/* IS NULL key */
2519				Assert(key->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHNULL);
2520
2521				if (!firstnull)
2522					break;		/* unsafe */
2523
2524				/* Safe, IS NULL key satisfied by every tuple */
2525				continue;
2526			}
2527			if (firstnull ||
2528				!DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(&key->sk_func,
2529												key->sk_collation, firstdatum,
2530												key->sk_argument)))
2531				break;			/* unsafe */
2532
2533			/* Safe, scalar = key satisfied by every tuple */
2534			continue;
2535		}
2536
2537		/* = array key (could be a SAOP array, could be a skip array) */
2538		array = &so->arrayKeys[arrayidx++];
2539		Assert(array->scan_key == startikey);
2540		if (array->num_elems != -1)
2541		{
2542			/*
2543			 * SAOP array = key.
2544			 *
2545			 * Handle this like we handle scalar = keys (though binary search
2546			 * for a matching element, to avoid relying on key's sk_argument).
2547			 */
2548			if (key->sk_attno >= firstchangingattnum)
2549				break;			/* unsafe, multiple distinct attr values */
2550
2551			firstdatum = index_getattr(firsttup, key->sk_attno, tupdesc,
2552									   &firstnull);
2553			_bt_binsrch_array_skey(&so->orderProcs[startikey],
2554								   false, NoMovementScanDirection,
2555								   firstdatum, firstnull, array, key,
2556								   &result);
2557			if (result != 0)
2558				break;			/* unsafe */
2559
2560			/* Safe, SAOP = key satisfied by every tuple */
2561			start_past_saop_eq = true;
2562			continue;
2563		}
2564
2565		/*
2566		 * Skip array = key
2567		 */
2568		Assert(key->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP);
2569		if (array->null_elem)
2570		{
2571			/*
2572			 * Non-range skip array = key.
2573			 *
2574			 * Safe, non-range skip array "satisfied" by every tuple on page
2575			 * (safe even when "key->sk_attno > firstchangingattnum").
2576			 */
2577			continue;
2578		}
2579
2580		/*
2581		 * Range skip array = key.
2582		 *
2583		 * Handle this like we handle scalar inequality keys (but avoid using
2584		 * key's sk_argument directly, as in the SAOP array case).
2585		 */
2586		if (key->sk_attno > firstchangingattnum)	/* >, not >= */
2587			break;				/* unsafe, preceding attr has multiple
2588								 * distinct values */
2589
2590		firstdatum = index_getattr(firsttup, key->sk_attno, tupdesc, &firstnull);
2591		lastdatum = index_getattr(lasttup, key->sk_attno, tupdesc, &lastnull);
2592
2593		/* Test firsttup */
2594		_bt_binsrch_skiparray_skey(false, ForwardScanDirection,
2595								   firstdatum, firstnull, array, key,
2596								   &result);
2597		if (result != 0)
2598			break;				/* unsafe */
2599
2600		/* Test lasttup */
2601		_bt_binsrch_skiparray_skey(false, ForwardScanDirection,
2602								   lastdatum, lastnull, array, key,
2603								   &result);
2604		if (result != 0)
2605			break;				/* unsafe */
2606
2607		/* Safe, range skip array satisfied by every tuple on page */
2608	}
2609
2610	/*
2611	 * Use of forcenonrequired is typically undesirable, since it'll force
2612	 * _bt_readpage caller to read every tuple on the page -- even though, in
2613	 * general, it might well be possible to end the scan on an earlier tuple.
2614	 * However, caller must use forcenonrequired when start_past_saop_eq=true,
2615	 * since the usual required array behavior might fail to roll over to the
2616	 * SAOP array.
2617	 *
2618	 * We always prefer forcenonrequired=true during scans with skip arrays
2619	 * (except on the first page of each primitive index scan), though -- even
2620	 * when "startikey == 0".  That way, _bt_advance_array_keys's low-order
2621	 * key precheck optimization can always be used (unless on the first page
2622	 * of the scan).  It seems slightly preferable to check more tuples when
2623	 * that allows us to do significantly less skip array maintenance.
2624	 */
2625	pstate->forcenonrequired = (start_past_saop_eq || so->skipScan);
2626	pstate->startikey = startikey;
2627
2628	/*
2629	 * _bt_readpage caller is required to call _bt_checkkeys against page's
2630	 * finaltup with forcenonrequired=false whenever we initially set
2631	 * forcenonrequired=true.  That way the scan's arrays will reliably track
2632	 * its progress through the index's key space.
2633	 *
2634	 * We don't expect this when _bt_readpage caller has no finaltup due to
2635	 * its page being the rightmost (or the leftmost, during backwards scans).
2636	 * When we see that _bt_readpage has no finaltup, back out of everything.
2637	 */
2638	Assert(!pstate->forcenonrequired || so->numArrayKeys);
2639	if (pstate->forcenonrequired && !pstate->finaltup)
2640	{
2641		pstate->forcenonrequired = false;
2642		pstate->startikey = 0;
2643	}
2644}
2645
2646/*
2647 * Test whether an indextuple satisfies current scan condition.
2648 *
2649 * Return true if so, false if not.  If not, also sets *continuescan to false
2650 * when it's also not possible for any later tuples to pass the current qual
2651 * (with the scan's current set of array keys, in the current scan direction),
2652 * in addition to setting *ikey to the so->keyData[] subscript/offset for the
2653 * unsatisfied scan key (needed when caller must consider advancing the scan's
2654 * array keys).
2655 *
2656 * This is a subroutine for _bt_checkkeys.  We provisionally assume that
2657 * reaching the end of the current set of required keys (in particular the
2658 * current required array keys) ends the ongoing (primitive) index scan.
2659 * Callers without array keys should just end the scan right away when they
2660 * find that continuescan has been set to false here by us.  Things are more
2661 * complicated for callers with array keys.
2662 *
2663 * Callers with array keys must first consider advancing the arrays when
2664 * continuescan has been set to false here by us.  They must then consider if
2665 * it really does make sense to end the current (primitive) index scan, in
2666 * light of everything that is known at that point.  (In general when we set
2667 * continuescan=false for these callers it must be treated as provisional.)
2668 *
2669 * We deal with advancing unsatisfied non-required arrays directly, though.
2670 * This is safe, since by definition non-required keys can't end the scan.
2671 * This is just how we determine if non-required arrays are just unsatisfied
2672 * by the current array key, or if they're truly unsatisfied (that is, if
2673 * they're unsatisfied by every possible array key).
2674 *
2675 * Pass advancenonrequired=false to avoid all array related side effects.
2676 * This allows _bt_advance_array_keys caller to avoid infinite recursion.
2677 *
2678 * Pass forcenonrequired=true to instruct us to treat all keys as nonrequired.
2679 * This is used to make it safe to temporarily stop properly maintaining the
2680 * scan's required arrays.  _bt_checkkeys caller (_bt_readpage, actually)
2681 * determines a prefix of keys that must satisfy every possible corresponding
2682 * index attribute value from its page, which is passed to us via *ikey arg
2683 * (this is the first key that might be unsatisfied by tuples on the page).
2684 * Obviously, we won't maintain any array keys from before *ikey, so it's
2685 * quite possible for such arrays to "fall behind" the index's keyspace.
2686 * Caller will need to "catch up" by passing forcenonrequired=true (alongside
2687 * an *ikey=0) once the page's finaltup is reached.
2688 *
2689 * Note: it's safe to pass an *ikey > 0 with forcenonrequired=false, but only
2690 * when caller determines that it won't affect array maintenance.
2691 */
2692static bool
2693_bt_check_compare(IndexScanDesc scan, ScanDirection dir,
2694				  IndexTuple tuple, int tupnatts, TupleDesc tupdesc,
2695				  bool advancenonrequired, bool forcenonrequired,
2696				  bool *continuescan, int *ikey)
2697{
2698	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
2699
2700	*continuescan = true;		/* default assumption */
2701
2702	for (; *ikey < so->numberOfKeys; (*ikey)++)
2703	{
2704		ScanKey		key = so->keyData + *ikey;
2705		Datum		datum;
2706		bool		isNull;
2707		bool		requiredSameDir = false,
2708					requiredOppositeDirOnly = false;
2709
2710		/*
2711		 * Check if the key is required in the current scan direction, in the
2712		 * opposite scan direction _only_, or in neither direction (except
2713		 * when we're forced to treat all scan keys as nonrequired)
2714		 */
2715		if (forcenonrequired)
2716		{
2717			/* treating scan's keys as non-required */
2718		}
2719		else if (((key->sk_flags & SK_BT_REQFWD) && ScanDirectionIsForward(dir)) ||
2720				 ((key->sk_flags & SK_BT_REQBKWD) && ScanDirectionIsBackward(dir)))
2721			requiredSameDir = true;
2722		else if (((key->sk_flags & SK_BT_REQFWD) && ScanDirectionIsBackward(dir)) ||
2723				 ((key->sk_flags & SK_BT_REQBKWD) && ScanDirectionIsForward(dir)))
2724			requiredOppositeDirOnly = true;
2725
2726		if (key->sk_attno > tupnatts)
2727		{
2728			/*
2729			 * This attribute is truncated (must be high key).  The value for
2730			 * this attribute in the first non-pivot tuple on the page to the
2731			 * right could be any possible value.  Assume that truncated
2732			 * attribute passes the qual.
2733			 */
2734			Assert(BTreeTupleIsPivot(tuple));
2735			continue;
2736		}
2737
2738		/*
2739		 * A skip array scan key uses one of several sentinel values.  We just
2740		 * fall back on _bt_tuple_before_array_skeys when we see such a value.
2741		 */
2742		if (key->sk_flags & (SK_BT_MINVAL | SK_BT_MAXVAL |
2743							 SK_BT_NEXT | SK_BT_PRIOR))
2744		{
2745			Assert(key->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY);
2746			Assert(key->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP);
2747			Assert(requiredSameDir || forcenonrequired);
2748
2749			/*
2750			 * Cannot fall back on _bt_tuple_before_array_skeys when we're
2751			 * treating the scan's keys as nonrequired, though.  Just handle
2752			 * this like any other non-required equality-type array key.
2753			 */
2754			if (forcenonrequired)
2755				return _bt_advance_array_keys(scan, NULL, tuple, tupnatts,
2756											  tupdesc, *ikey, false);
2757
2758			*continuescan = false;
2759			return false;
2760		}
2761
2762		/* row-comparison keys need special processing */
2763		if (key->sk_flags & SK_ROW_HEADER)
2764		{
2765			if (_bt_check_rowcompare(key, tuple, tupnatts, tupdesc, dir,
2766									 forcenonrequired, continuescan))
2767				continue;
2768			return false;
2769		}
2770
2771		datum = index_getattr(tuple,
2772							  key->sk_attno,
2773							  tupdesc,
2774							  &isNull);
2775
2776		if (key->sk_flags & SK_ISNULL)
2777		{
2778			/* Handle IS NULL/NOT NULL tests */
2779			if (key->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHNULL)
2780			{
2781				if (isNull)
2782					continue;	/* tuple satisfies this qual */
2783			}
2784			else
2785			{
2786				Assert(key->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHNOTNULL);
2787				Assert(!(key->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP));
2788				if (!isNull)
2789					continue;	/* tuple satisfies this qual */
2790			}
2791
2792			/*
2793			 * Tuple fails this qual.  If it's a required qual for the current
2794			 * scan direction, then we can conclude no further tuples will
2795			 * pass, either.
2796			 */
2797			if (requiredSameDir)
2798				*continuescan = false;
2799			else if (unlikely(key->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP))
2800			{
2801				/*
2802				 * If we're treating scan keys as nonrequired, and encounter a
2803				 * skip array scan key whose current element is NULL, then it
2804				 * must be a non-range skip array.  It must be satisfied, so
2805				 * there's no need to call _bt_advance_array_keys to check.
2806				 */
2807				Assert(forcenonrequired && *ikey > 0);
2808				continue;
2809			}
2810
2811			/*
2812			 * This indextuple doesn't match the qual.
2813			 */
2814			return false;
2815		}
2816
2817		if (isNull)
2818		{
2819			/*
2820			 * Scalar scan key isn't satisfied by NULL tuple value.
2821			 *
2822			 * If we're treating scan keys as nonrequired, and key is for a
2823			 * skip array, then we must attempt to advance the array to NULL
2824			 * (if we're successful then the tuple might match the qual).
2825			 */
2826			if (unlikely(forcenonrequired && key->sk_flags & SK_BT_SKIP))
2827				return _bt_advance_array_keys(scan, NULL, tuple, tupnatts,
2828											  tupdesc, *ikey, false);
2829
2830			if (key->sk_flags & SK_BT_NULLS_FIRST)
2831			{
2832				/*
2833				 * Since NULLs are sorted before non-NULLs, we know we have
2834				 * reached the lower limit of the range of values for this
2835				 * index attr.  On a backward scan, we can stop if this qual
2836				 * is one of the "must match" subset.  We can stop regardless
2837				 * of whether the qual is > or <, so long as it's required,
2838				 * because it's not possible for any future tuples to pass. On
2839				 * a forward scan, however, we must keep going, because we may
2840				 * have initially positioned to the start of the index.
2841				 * (_bt_advance_array_keys also relies on this behavior during
2842				 * forward scans.)
2843				 */
2844				if ((requiredSameDir || requiredOppositeDirOnly) &&
2845					ScanDirectionIsBackward(dir))
2846					*continuescan = false;
2847			}
2848			else
2849			{
2850				/*
2851				 * Since NULLs are sorted after non-NULLs, we know we have
2852				 * reached the upper limit of the range of values for this
2853				 * index attr.  On a forward scan, we can stop if this qual is
2854				 * one of the "must match" subset.  We can stop regardless of
2855				 * whether the qual is > or <, so long as it's required,
2856				 * because it's not possible for any future tuples to pass. On
2857				 * a backward scan, however, we must keep going, because we
2858				 * may have initially positioned to the end of the index.
2859				 * (_bt_advance_array_keys also relies on this behavior during
2860				 * backward scans.)
2861				 */
2862				if ((requiredSameDir || requiredOppositeDirOnly) &&
2863					ScanDirectionIsForward(dir))
2864					*continuescan = false;
2865			}
2866
2867			/*
2868			 * This indextuple doesn't match the qual.
2869			 */
2870			return false;
2871		}
2872
2873		if (!DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(&key->sk_func, key->sk_collation,
2874											datum, key->sk_argument)))
2875		{
2876			/*
2877			 * Tuple fails this qual.  If it's a required qual for the current
2878			 * scan direction, then we can conclude no further tuples will
2879			 * pass, either.
2880			 */
2881			if (requiredSameDir)
2882				*continuescan = false;
2883
2884			/*
2885			 * If this is a non-required equality-type array key, the tuple
2886			 * needs to be checked against every possible array key.  Handle
2887			 * this by "advancing" the scan key's array to a matching value
2888			 * (if we're successful then the tuple might match the qual).
2889			 */
2890			else if (advancenonrequired &&
2891					 key->sk_strategy == BTEqualStrategyNumber &&
2892					 (key->sk_flags & SK_SEARCHARRAY))
2893				return _bt_advance_array_keys(scan, NULL, tuple, tupnatts,
2894											  tupdesc, *ikey, false);
2895
2896			/*
2897			 * This indextuple doesn't match the qual.
2898			 */
2899			return false;
2900		}
2901	}
2902
2903	/* If we get here, the tuple passes all index quals. */
2904	return true;
2905}
2906
2907/*
2908 * Test whether an indextuple satisfies a row-comparison scan condition.
2909 *
2910 * Return true if so, false if not.  If not, also clear *continuescan if
2911 * it's not possible for any future tuples in the current scan direction
2912 * to pass the qual.
2913 *
2914 * This is a subroutine for _bt_checkkeys/_bt_check_compare.  Caller passes us
2915 * a row compare header key taken from so->keyData[].
2916 *
2917 * Row value comparisons can be described in terms of logical expansions that
2918 * use only scalar operators.  Consider the following example row comparison:
2919 *
2920 * "(a, b, c) > (7, 'bar', 62)"
2921 *
2922 * This can be evaluated as:
2923 *
2924 * "(a = 7 AND b = 'bar' AND c > 62) OR (a = 7 AND b > 'bar') OR (a > 7)".
2925 *
2926 * Notice that this condition is satisfied by _all_ rows that satisfy "a > 7",
2927 * and by a subset of all rows that satisfy "a >= 7" (possibly all such rows).
2928 * It _can't_ be satisfied by other rows (where "a < 7" or where "a IS NULL").
2929 * A row comparison header key can therefore often be treated as if it was a
2930 * simple scalar inequality on the row compare's most significant column.
2931 * (For example, _bt_advance_array_keys and most preprocessing routines treat
2932 * row compares like any other same-strategy inequality on the same column.)
2933 *
2934 * Things get more complicated for our row compare given a row where "a = 7".
2935 * Note that a row compare isn't necessarily satisfied by _every_ tuple that
2936 * appears between the first and last satisfied tuple returned by the scan,
2937 * due to the way that its lower-order subkeys are only conditionally applied.
2938 * A forwards scan that uses our example qual might initially return a tuple
2939 * "(a, b, c) = (7, 'zebra', 54)".  But it won't subsequently return a tuple
2940 * "(a, b, c) = (7, NULL, 1)" located to the right of the first matching tuple
2941 * (assume that "b" was declared NULLS LAST here).  The scan will only return
2942 * additional matches upon reaching tuples where "a > 7".  If you rereview our
2943 * example row comparison's logical expansion, you'll understand why this is.
2944 * (Here we assume that all subkeys could be marked required, guaranteeing
2945 * that row comparison order matches index order.  This is the common case.)
2946 *
2947 * Note that a row comparison header key behaves _exactly_ the same as a
2948 * similar scalar inequality key on the row's most significant column once the
2949 * scan reaches the point where it no longer needs to evaluate lower-order
2950 * subkeys (or before the point where it starts needing to evaluate them).
2951 * For example, once a forwards scan that uses our example qual reaches the
2952 * first tuple "a > 7", we'll behave in just the same way as our caller would
2953 * behave with a similar scalar inequality "a > 7" for the remainder of the
2954 * scan (assuming that the scan never changes direction/never goes backwards).
2955 * We'll even set continuescan=false according to exactly the same rules as
2956 * the ones our caller applies with simple scalar inequalities, including the
2957 * rules it applies when NULL tuple values don't satisfy an inequality qual.
2958 */
2959static bool
2960_bt_check_rowcompare(ScanKey header, IndexTuple tuple, int tupnatts,
2961					 TupleDesc tupdesc, ScanDirection dir,
2962					 bool forcenonrequired, bool *continuescan)
2963{
2964	ScanKey		subkey = (ScanKey) DatumGetPointer(header->sk_argument);
2965	int32		cmpresult = 0;
2966	bool		result;
2967
2968	/* First subkey should be same as the header says */
2969	Assert(header->sk_flags & SK_ROW_HEADER);
2970	Assert(subkey->sk_attno == header->sk_attno);
2971	Assert(subkey->sk_strategy == header->sk_strategy);
2972
2973	/* Loop over columns of the row condition */
2974	for (;;)
2975	{
2976		Datum		datum;
2977		bool		isNull;
2978
2979		Assert(subkey->sk_flags & SK_ROW_MEMBER);
2980
2981		/* When a NULL row member is compared, the row never matches */
2982		if (subkey->sk_flags & SK_ISNULL)
2983		{
2984			/*
2985			 * Unlike the simple-scankey case, this isn't a disallowed case
2986			 * (except when it's the first row element that has the NULL arg).
2987			 * But it can never match.  If all the earlier row comparison
2988			 * columns are required for the scan direction, we can stop the
2989			 * scan, because there can't be another tuple that will succeed.
2990			 */
2991			Assert(subkey != (ScanKey) DatumGetPointer(header->sk_argument));
2992			subkey--;
2993			if (forcenonrequired)
2994			{
2995				/* treating scan's keys as non-required */
2996			}
2997			else if ((subkey->sk_flags & SK_BT_REQFWD) &&
2998					 ScanDirectionIsForward(dir))
2999				*continuescan = false;
3000			else if ((subkey->sk_flags & SK_BT_REQBKWD) &&
3001					 ScanDirectionIsBackward(dir))
3002				*continuescan = false;
3003			return false;
3004		}
3005
3006		if (subkey->sk_attno > tupnatts)
3007		{
3008			/*
3009			 * This attribute is truncated (must be high key).  The value for
3010			 * this attribute in the first non-pivot tuple on the page to the
3011			 * right could be any possible value.  Assume that truncated
3012			 * attribute passes the qual.
3013			 */
3014			Assert(BTreeTupleIsPivot(tuple));
3015			return true;
3016		}
3017
3018		datum = index_getattr(tuple,
3019							  subkey->sk_attno,
3020							  tupdesc,
3021							  &isNull);
3022
3023		if (isNull)
3024		{
3025			int			reqflags;
3026
3027			if (forcenonrequired)
3028			{
3029				/* treating scan's keys as non-required */
3030			}
3031			else if (subkey->sk_flags & SK_BT_NULLS_FIRST)
3032			{
3033				/*
3034				 * Since NULLs are sorted before non-NULLs, we know we have
3035				 * reached the lower limit of the range of values for this
3036				 * index attr.  On a backward scan, we can stop if this qual
3037				 * is one of the "must match" subset.  However, on a forwards
3038				 * scan, we must keep going, because we may have initially
3039				 * positioned to the start of the index.
3040				 *
3041				 * All required NULLS FIRST > row members can use NULL tuple
3042				 * values to end backwards scans, just like with other values.
3043				 * A qual "WHERE (a, b, c) > (9, 42, 'foo')" can terminate a
3044				 * backwards scan upon reaching the index's rightmost "a = 9"
3045				 * tuple whose "b" column contains a NULL (if not sooner).
3046				 * Since "b" is NULLS FIRST, we can treat its NULLs as "<" 42.
3047				 */
3048				reqflags = SK_BT_REQBKWD;
3049
3050				/*
3051				 * When a most significant required NULLS FIRST < row compare
3052				 * member sees NULL tuple values during a backwards scan, it
3053				 * signals the end of matches for the whole row compare/scan.
3054				 * A qual "WHERE (a, b, c) < (9, 42, 'foo')" will terminate a
3055				 * backwards scan upon reaching the rightmost tuple whose "a"
3056				 * column has a NULL.  The "a" NULL value is "<" 9, and yet
3057				 * our < row compare will still end the scan.  (This isn't
3058				 * safe with later/lower-order row members.  Notice that it
3059				 * can only happen with an "a" NULL some time after the scan
3060				 * completely stops needing to use its "b" and "c" members.)
3061				 */
3062				if (subkey == (ScanKey) DatumGetPointer(header->sk_argument))
3063					reqflags |= SK_BT_REQFWD;	/* safe, first row member */
3064
3065				if ((subkey->sk_flags & reqflags) &&
3066					ScanDirectionIsBackward(dir))
3067					*continuescan = false;
3068			}
3069			else
3070			{
3071				/*
3072				 * Since NULLs are sorted after non-NULLs, we know we have
3073				 * reached the upper limit of the range of values for this
3074				 * index attr.  On a forward scan, we can stop if this qual is
3075				 * one of the "must match" subset.  However, on a backward
3076				 * scan, we must keep going, because we may have initially
3077				 * positioned to the end of the index.
3078				 *
3079				 * All required NULLS LAST < row members can use NULL tuple
3080				 * values to end forwards scans, just like with other values.
3081				 * A qual "WHERE (a, b, c) < (9, 42, 'foo')" can terminate a
3082				 * forwards scan upon reaching the index's leftmost "a = 9"
3083				 * tuple whose "b" column contains a NULL (if not sooner).
3084				 * Since "b" is NULLS LAST, we can treat its NULLs as ">" 42.
3085				 */
3086				reqflags = SK_BT_REQFWD;
3087
3088				/*
3089				 * When a most significant required NULLS LAST > row compare
3090				 * member sees NULL tuple values during a forwards scan, it
3091				 * signals the end of matches for the whole row compare/scan.
3092				 * A qual "WHERE (a, b, c) > (9, 42, 'foo')" will terminate a
3093				 * forwards scan upon reaching the leftmost tuple whose "a"
3094				 * column has a NULL.  The "a" NULL value is ">" 9, and yet
3095				 * our > row compare will end the scan.  (This isn't safe with
3096				 * later/lower-order row members.  Notice that it can only
3097				 * happen with an "a" NULL some time after the scan completely
3098				 * stops needing to use its "b" and "c" members.)
3099				 */
3100				if (subkey == (ScanKey) DatumGetPointer(header->sk_argument))
3101					reqflags |= SK_BT_REQBKWD;	/* safe, first row member */
3102
3103				if ((subkey->sk_flags & reqflags) &&
3104					ScanDirectionIsForward(dir))
3105					*continuescan = false;
3106			}
3107
3108			/*
3109			 * In any case, this indextuple doesn't match the qual.
3110			 */
3111			return false;
3112		}
3113
3114		/* Perform the test --- three-way comparison not bool operator */
3115		cmpresult = DatumGetInt32(FunctionCall2Coll(&subkey->sk_func,
3116													subkey->sk_collation,
3117													datum,
3118													subkey->sk_argument));
3119
3120		if (subkey->sk_flags & SK_BT_DESC)
3121			INVERT_COMPARE_RESULT(cmpresult);
3122
3123		/* Done comparing if unequal, else advance to next column */
3124		if (cmpresult != 0)
3125			break;
3126
3127		if (subkey->sk_flags & SK_ROW_END)
3128			break;
3129		subkey++;
3130	}
3131
3132	/*
3133	 * At this point cmpresult indicates the overall result of the row
3134	 * comparison, and subkey points to the deciding column (or the last
3135	 * column if the result is "=").
3136	 */
3137	switch (subkey->sk_strategy)
3138	{
3139			/* EQ and NE cases aren't allowed here */
3140		case BTLessStrategyNumber:
3141			result = (cmpresult < 0);
3142			break;
3143		case BTLessEqualStrategyNumber:
3144			result = (cmpresult <= 0);
3145			break;
3146		case BTGreaterEqualStrategyNumber:
3147			result = (cmpresult >= 0);
3148			break;
3149		case BTGreaterStrategyNumber:
3150			result = (cmpresult > 0);
3151			break;
3152		default:
3153			elog(ERROR, "unexpected strategy number %d", subkey->sk_strategy);
3154			result = 0;			/* keep compiler quiet */
3155			break;
3156	}
3157
3158	if (!result && !forcenonrequired)
3159	{
3160		/*
3161		 * Tuple fails this qual.  If it's a required qual for the current
3162		 * scan direction, then we can conclude no further tuples will pass,
3163		 * either.  Note we have to look at the deciding column, not
3164		 * necessarily the first or last column of the row condition.
3165		 */
3166		if ((subkey->sk_flags & SK_BT_REQFWD) &&
3167			ScanDirectionIsForward(dir))
3168			*continuescan = false;
3169		else if ((subkey->sk_flags & SK_BT_REQBKWD) &&
3170				 ScanDirectionIsBackward(dir))
3171			*continuescan = false;
3172	}
3173
3174	return result;
3175}
3176
3177/*
3178 * Determine if a scan with array keys should skip over uninteresting tuples.
3179 *
3180 * This is a subroutine for _bt_checkkeys.  Called when _bt_readpage's linear
3181 * search process (started after it finishes reading an initial group of
3182 * matching tuples, used to locate the start of the next group of tuples
3183 * matching the next set of required array keys) has already scanned an
3184 * excessive number of tuples whose key space is "between arrays".
3185 *
3186 * When we perform look ahead successfully, we'll sets pstate.skip, which
3187 * instructs _bt_readpage to skip ahead to that tuple next (could be past the
3188 * end of the scan's leaf page).  Pages where the optimization is effective
3189 * will generally still need to skip several times.  Each call here performs
3190 * only a single "look ahead" comparison of a later tuple, whose distance from
3191 * the current tuple's offset number is determined by applying heuristics.
3192 */
3193static void
3194_bt_checkkeys_look_ahead(IndexScanDesc scan, BTReadPageState *pstate,
3195						 int tupnatts, TupleDesc tupdesc)
3196{
3197	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
3198	ScanDirection dir = so->currPos.dir;
3199	OffsetNumber aheadoffnum;
3200	IndexTuple	ahead;
3201
3202	Assert(!pstate->forcenonrequired);
3203
3204	/* Avoid looking ahead when comparing the page high key */
3205	if (pstate->offnum < pstate->minoff)
3206		return;
3207
3208	/*
3209	 * Don't look ahead when there aren't enough tuples remaining on the page
3210	 * (in the current scan direction) for it to be worth our while
3211	 */
3212	if (ScanDirectionIsForward(dir) &&
3213		pstate->offnum >= pstate->maxoff - LOOK_AHEAD_DEFAULT_DISTANCE)
3214		return;
3215	else if (ScanDirectionIsBackward(dir) &&
3216			 pstate->offnum <= pstate->minoff + LOOK_AHEAD_DEFAULT_DISTANCE)
3217		return;
3218
3219	/*
3220	 * The look ahead distance starts small, and ramps up as each call here
3221	 * allows _bt_readpage to skip over more tuples
3222	 */
3223	if (!pstate->targetdistance)
3224		pstate->targetdistance = LOOK_AHEAD_DEFAULT_DISTANCE;
3225	else if (pstate->targetdistance < MaxIndexTuplesPerPage / 2)
3226		pstate->targetdistance *= 2;
3227
3228	/* Don't read past the end (or before the start) of the page, though */
3229	if (ScanDirectionIsForward(dir))
3230		aheadoffnum = Min((int) pstate->maxoff,
3231						  (int) pstate->offnum + pstate->targetdistance);
3232	else
3233		aheadoffnum = Max((int) pstate->minoff,
3234						  (int) pstate->offnum - pstate->targetdistance);
3235
3236	ahead = (IndexTuple) PageGetItem(pstate->page,
3237									 PageGetItemId(pstate->page, aheadoffnum));
3238	if (_bt_tuple_before_array_skeys(scan, dir, ahead, tupdesc, tupnatts,
3239									 false, 0, NULL))
3240	{
3241		/*
3242		 * Success -- instruct _bt_readpage to skip ahead to very next tuple
3243		 * after the one we determined was still before the current array keys
3244		 */
3245		if (ScanDirectionIsForward(dir))
3246			pstate->skip = aheadoffnum + 1;
3247		else
3248			pstate->skip = aheadoffnum - 1;
3249	}
3250	else
3251	{
3252		/*
3253		 * Failure -- "ahead" tuple is too far ahead (we were too aggressive).
3254		 *
3255		 * Reset the number of rechecks, and aggressively reduce the target
3256		 * distance (we're much more aggressive here than we were when the
3257		 * distance was initially ramped up).
3258		 */
3259		pstate->rechecks = 0;
3260		pstate->targetdistance = Max(pstate->targetdistance / 8, 1);
3261	}
3262}
3263
3264/*
3265 * _bt_killitems - set LP_DEAD state for items an indexscan caller has
3266 * told us were killed
3267 *
3268 * scan->opaque, referenced locally through so, contains information about the
3269 * current page and killed tuples thereon (generally, this should only be
3270 * called if so->numKilled > 0).
3271 *
3272 * Caller should not have a lock on the so->currPos page, but must hold a
3273 * buffer pin when !so->dropPin.  When we return, it still won't be locked.
3274 * It'll continue to hold whatever pins were held before calling here.
3275 *
3276 * We match items by heap TID before assuming they are the right ones to set
3277 * LP_DEAD.  If the scan is one that holds a buffer pin on the target page
3278 * continuously from initially reading the items until applying this function
3279 * (if it is a !so->dropPin scan), VACUUM cannot have deleted any items on the
3280 * page, so the page's TIDs can't have been recycled by now.  There's no risk
3281 * that we'll confuse a new index tuple that happens to use a recycled TID
3282 * with a now-removed tuple with the same TID (that used to be on this same
3283 * page).  We can't rely on that during scans that drop buffer pins eagerly
3284 * (so->dropPin scans), though, so we must condition setting LP_DEAD bits on
3285 * the page LSN having not changed since back when _bt_readpage saw the page.
3286 * We totally give up on setting LP_DEAD bits when the page LSN changed.
3287 *
3288 * We give up much less often during !so->dropPin scans, but it still happens.
3289 * We cope with cases where items have moved right due to insertions.  If an
3290 * item has moved off the current page due to a split, we'll fail to find it
3291 * and just give up on it.
3292 */
3293void
3294_bt_killitems(IndexScanDesc scan)
3295{
3296	Relation	rel = scan->indexRelation;
3297	BTScanOpaque so = (BTScanOpaque) scan->opaque;
3298	Page		page;
3299	BTPageOpaque opaque;
3300	OffsetNumber minoff;
3301	OffsetNumber maxoff;
3302	int			numKilled = so->numKilled;
3303	bool		killedsomething = false;
3304	Buffer		buf;
3305
3306	Assert(numKilled > 0);
3307	Assert(BTScanPosIsValid(so->currPos));
3308	Assert(scan->heapRelation != NULL); /* can't be a bitmap index scan */
3309
3310	/* Always invalidate so->killedItems[] before leaving so->currPos */
3311	so->numKilled = 0;
3312
3313	if (!so->dropPin)
3314	{
3315		/*
3316		 * We have held the pin on this page since we read the index tuples,
3317		 * so all we need to do is lock it.  The pin will have prevented
3318		 * concurrent VACUUMs from recycling any of the TIDs on the page.
3319		 */
3320		Assert(BTScanPosIsPinned(so->currPos));
3321		buf = so->currPos.buf;
3322		_bt_lockbuf(rel, buf, BT_READ);
3323	}
3324	else
3325	{
3326		XLogRecPtr	latestlsn;
3327
3328		Assert(!BTScanPosIsPinned(so->currPos));
3329		Assert(RelationNeedsWAL(rel));
3330		buf = _bt_getbuf(rel, so->currPos.currPage, BT_READ);
3331
3332		latestlsn = BufferGetLSNAtomic(buf);
3333		Assert(so->currPos.lsn <= latestlsn);
3334		if (so->currPos.lsn != latestlsn)
3335		{
3336			/* Modified, give up on hinting */
3337			_bt_relbuf(rel, buf);
3338			return;
3339		}
3340
3341		/* Unmodified, hinting is safe */
3342	}
3343
3344	page = BufferGetPage(buf);
3345	opaque = BTPageGetOpaque(page);
3346	minoff = P_FIRSTDATAKEY(opaque);
3347	maxoff = PageGetMaxOffsetNumber(page);
3348
3349	for (int i = 0; i < numKilled; i++)
3350	{
3351		int			itemIndex = so->killedItems[i];
3352		BTScanPosItem *kitem = &so->currPos.items[itemIndex];
3353		OffsetNumber offnum = kitem->indexOffset;
3354
3355		Assert(itemIndex >= so->currPos.firstItem &&
3356			   itemIndex <= so->currPos.lastItem);
3357		if (offnum < minoff)
3358			continue;			/* pure paranoia */
3359		while (offnum <= maxoff)
3360		{
3361			ItemId		iid = PageGetItemId(page, offnum);
3362			IndexTuple	ituple = (IndexTuple) PageGetItem(page, iid);
3363			bool		killtuple = false;
3364
3365			if (BTreeTupleIsPosting(ituple))
3366			{
3367				int			pi = i + 1;
3368				int			nposting = BTreeTupleGetNPosting(ituple);
3369				int			j;
3370
3371				/*
3372				 * We rely on the convention that heap TIDs in the scanpos
3373				 * items array are stored in ascending heap TID order for a
3374				 * group of TIDs that originally came from a posting list
3375				 * tuple.  This convention even applies during backwards
3376				 * scans, where returning the TIDs in descending order might
3377				 * seem more natural.  This is about effectiveness, not
3378				 * correctness.
3379				 *
3380				 * Note that the page may have been modified in almost any way
3381				 * since we first read it (in the !so->dropPin case), so it's
3382				 * possible that this posting list tuple wasn't a posting list
3383				 * tuple when we first encountered its heap TIDs.
3384				 */
3385				for (j = 0; j < nposting; j++)
3386				{
3387					ItemPointer item = BTreeTupleGetPostingN(ituple, j);
3388
3389					if (!ItemPointerEquals(item, &kitem->heapTid))
3390						break;	/* out of posting list loop */
3391
3392					/*
3393					 * kitem must have matching offnum when heap TIDs match,
3394					 * though only in the common case where the page can't
3395					 * have been concurrently modified
3396					 */
3397					Assert(kitem->indexOffset == offnum || !so->dropPin);
3398
3399					/*
3400					 * Read-ahead to later kitems here.
3401					 *
3402					 * We rely on the assumption that not advancing kitem here
3403					 * will prevent us from considering the posting list tuple
3404					 * fully dead by not matching its next heap TID in next
3405					 * loop iteration.
3406					 *
3407					 * If, on the other hand, this is the final heap TID in
3408					 * the posting list tuple, then tuple gets killed
3409					 * regardless (i.e. we handle the case where the last
3410					 * kitem is also the last heap TID in the last index tuple
3411					 * correctly -- posting tuple still gets killed).
3412					 */
3413					if (pi < numKilled)
3414						kitem = &so->currPos.items[so->killedItems[pi++]];
3415				}
3416
3417				/*
3418				 * Don't bother advancing the outermost loop's int iterator to
3419				 * avoid processing killed items that relate to the same
3420				 * offnum/posting list tuple.  This micro-optimization hardly
3421				 * seems worth it.  (Further iterations of the outermost loop
3422				 * will fail to match on this same posting list's first heap
3423				 * TID instead, so we'll advance to the next offnum/index
3424				 * tuple pretty quickly.)
3425				 */
3426				if (j == nposting)
3427					killtuple = true;
3428			}
3429			else if (ItemPointerEquals(&ituple->t_tid, &kitem->heapTid))
3430				killtuple = true;
3431
3432			/*
3433			 * Mark index item as dead, if it isn't already.  Since this
3434			 * happens while holding a buffer lock possibly in shared mode,
3435			 * it's possible that multiple processes attempt to do this
3436			 * simultaneously, leading to multiple full-page images being sent
3437			 * to WAL (if wal_log_hints or data checksums are enabled), which
3438			 * is undesirable.
3439			 */
3440			if (killtuple && !ItemIdIsDead(iid))
3441			{
3442				/* found the item/all posting list items */
3443				ItemIdMarkDead(iid);
3444				killedsomething = true;
3445				break;			/* out of inner search loop */
3446			}
3447			offnum = OffsetNumberNext(offnum);
3448		}
3449	}
3450
3451	/*
3452	 * Since this can be redone later if needed, mark as dirty hint.
3453	 *
3454	 * Whenever we mark anything LP_DEAD, we also set the page's
3455	 * BTP_HAS_GARBAGE flag, which is likewise just a hint.  (Note that we
3456	 * only rely on the page-level flag in !heapkeyspace indexes.)
3457	 */
3458	if (killedsomething)
3459	{
3460		opaque->btpo_flags |= BTP_HAS_GARBAGE;
3461		MarkBufferDirtyHint(buf, true);
3462	}
3463
3464	if (!so->dropPin)
3465		_bt_unlockbuf(rel, buf);
3466	else
3467		_bt_relbuf(rel, buf);
3468}
3469
3470
3471/*
3472 * The following routines manage a shared-memory area in which we track
3473 * assignment of "vacuum cycle IDs" to currently-active btree vacuuming
3474 * operations.  There is a single counter which increments each time we
3475 * start a vacuum to assign it a cycle ID.  Since multiple vacuums could
3476 * be active concurrently, we have to track the cycle ID for each active
3477 * vacuum; this requires at most MaxBackends entries (usually far fewer).
3478 * We assume at most one vacuum can be active for a given index.
3479 *
3480 * Access to the shared memory area is controlled by BtreeVacuumLock.
3481 * In principle we could use a separate lmgr locktag for each index,
3482 * but a single LWLock is much cheaper, and given the short time that
3483 * the lock is ever held, the concurrency hit should be minimal.
3484 */
3485
3486typedef struct BTOneVacInfo
3487{
3488	LockRelId	relid;			/* global identifier of an index */
3489	BTCycleId	cycleid;		/* cycle ID for its active VACUUM */
3490} BTOneVacInfo;
3491
3492typedef struct BTVacInfo
3493{
3494	BTCycleId	cycle_ctr;		/* cycle ID most recently assigned */
3495	int			num_vacuums;	/* number of currently active VACUUMs */
3496	int			max_vacuums;	/* allocated length of vacuums[] array */
3497	BTOneVacInfo vacuums[FLEXIBLE_ARRAY_MEMBER];
3498} BTVacInfo;
3499
3500static BTVacInfo *btvacinfo;
3501
3502
3503/*
3504 * _bt_vacuum_cycleid --- get the active vacuum cycle ID for an index,
3505 *		or zero if there is no active VACUUM
3506 *
3507 * Note: for correct interlocking, the caller must already hold pin and
3508 * exclusive lock on each buffer it will store the cycle ID into.  This
3509 * ensures that even if a VACUUM starts immediately afterwards, it cannot
3510 * process those pages until the page split is complete.
3511 */
3512BTCycleId
3513_bt_vacuum_cycleid(Relation rel)
3514{
3515	BTCycleId	result = 0;
3516	int			i;
3517
3518	/* Share lock is enough since this is a read-only operation */
3519	LWLockAcquire(BtreeVacuumLock, LW_SHARED);
3520
3521	for (i = 0; i < btvacinfo->num_vacuums; i++)
3522	{
3523		BTOneVacInfo *vac = &btvacinfo->vacuums[i];
3524
3525		if (vac->relid.relId == rel->rd_lockInfo.lockRelId.relId &&
3526			vac->relid.dbId == rel->rd_lockInfo.lockRelId.dbId)
3527		{
3528			result = vac->cycleid;
3529			break;
3530		}
3531	}
3532
3533	LWLockRelease(BtreeVacuumLock);
3534	return result;
3535}
3536
3537/*
3538 * _bt_start_vacuum --- assign a cycle ID to a just-starting VACUUM operation
3539 *
3540 * Note: the caller must guarantee that it will eventually call
3541 * _bt_end_vacuum, else we'll permanently leak an array slot.  To ensure
3542 * that this happens even in elog(FATAL) scenarios, the appropriate coding
3543 * is not just a PG_TRY, but
3544 *		PG_ENSURE_ERROR_CLEANUP(_bt_end_vacuum_callback, PointerGetDatum(rel))
3545 */
3546BTCycleId
3547_bt_start_vacuum(Relation rel)
3548{
3549	BTCycleId	result;
3550	int			i;
3551	BTOneVacInfo *vac;
3552
3553	LWLockAcquire(BtreeVacuumLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
3554
3555	/*
3556	 * Assign the next cycle ID, being careful to avoid zero as well as the
3557	 * reserved high values.
3558	 */
3559	result = ++(btvacinfo->cycle_ctr);
3560	if (result == 0 || result > MAX_BT_CYCLE_ID)
3561		result = btvacinfo->cycle_ctr = 1;
3562
3563	/* Let's just make sure there's no entry already for this index */
3564	for (i = 0; i < btvacinfo->num_vacuums; i++)
3565	{
3566		vac = &btvacinfo->vacuums[i];
3567		if (vac->relid.relId == rel->rd_lockInfo.lockRelId.relId &&
3568			vac->relid.dbId == rel->rd_lockInfo.lockRelId.dbId)
3569		{
3570			/*
3571			 * Unlike most places in the backend, we have to explicitly
3572			 * release our LWLock before throwing an error.  This is because
3573			 * we expect _bt_end_vacuum() to be called before transaction
3574			 * abort cleanup can run to release LWLocks.
3575			 */
3576			LWLockRelease(BtreeVacuumLock);
3577			elog(ERROR, "multiple active vacuums for index \"%s\"",
3578				 RelationGetRelationName(rel));
3579		}
3580	}
3581
3582	/* OK, add an entry */
3583	if (btvacinfo->num_vacuums >= btvacinfo->max_vacuums)
3584	{
3585		LWLockRelease(BtreeVacuumLock);
3586		elog(ERROR, "out of btvacinfo slots");
3587	}
3588	vac = &btvacinfo->vacuums[btvacinfo->num_vacuums];
3589	vac->relid = rel->rd_lockInfo.lockRelId;
3590	vac->cycleid = result;
3591	btvacinfo->num_vacuums++;
3592
3593	LWLockRelease(BtreeVacuumLock);
3594	return result;
3595}
3596
3597/*
3598 * _bt_end_vacuum --- mark a btree VACUUM operation as done
3599 *
3600 * Note: this is deliberately coded not to complain if no entry is found;
3601 * this allows the caller to put PG_TRY around the start_vacuum operation.
3602 */
3603void
3604_bt_end_vacuum(Relation rel)
3605{
3606	int			i;
3607
3608	LWLockAcquire(BtreeVacuumLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
3609
3610	/* Find the array entry */
3611	for (i = 0; i < btvacinfo->num_vacuums; i++)
3612	{
3613		BTOneVacInfo *vac = &btvacinfo->vacuums[i];
3614
3615		if (vac->relid.relId == rel->rd_lockInfo.lockRelId.relId &&
3616			vac->relid.dbId == rel->rd_lockInfo.lockRelId.dbId)
3617		{
3618			/* Remove it by shifting down the last entry */
3619			*vac = btvacinfo->vacuums[btvacinfo->num_vacuums - 1];
3620			btvacinfo->num_vacuums--;
3621			break;
3622		}
3623	}
3624
3625	LWLockRelease(BtreeVacuumLock);
3626}
3627
3628/*
3629 * _bt_end_vacuum wrapped as an on_shmem_exit callback function
3630 */
3631void
3632_bt_end_vacuum_callback(int code, Datum arg)
3633{
3634	_bt_end_vacuum((Relation) DatumGetPointer(arg));
3635}
3636
3637/*
3638 * BTreeShmemSize --- report amount of shared memory space needed
3639 */
3640Size
3641BTreeShmemSize(void)
3642{
3643	Size		size;
3644
3645	size = offsetof(BTVacInfo, vacuums);
3646	size = add_size(size, mul_size(MaxBackends, sizeof(BTOneVacInfo)));
3647	return size;
3648}
3649
3650/*
3651 * BTreeShmemInit --- initialize this module's shared memory
3652 */
3653void
3654BTreeShmemInit(void)
3655{
3656	bool		found;
3657
3658	btvacinfo = (BTVacInfo *) ShmemInitStruct("BTree Vacuum State",
3659											  BTreeShmemSize(),
3660											  &found);
3661
3662	if (!IsUnderPostmaster)
3663	{
3664		/* Initialize shared memory area */
3665		Assert(!found);
3666
3667		/*
3668		 * It doesn't really matter what the cycle counter starts at, but
3669		 * having it always start the same doesn't seem good.  Seed with
3670		 * low-order bits of time() instead.
3671		 */
3672		btvacinfo->cycle_ctr = (BTCycleId) time(NULL);
3673
3674		btvacinfo->num_vacuums = 0;
3675		btvacinfo->max_vacuums = MaxBackends;
3676	}
3677	else
3678		Assert(found);
3679}
3680
3681bytea *
3682btoptions(Datum reloptions, bool validate)
3683{
3684	static const relopt_parse_elt tab[] = {
3685		{"fillfactor", RELOPT_TYPE_INT, offsetof(BTOptions, fillfactor)},
3686		{"vacuum_cleanup_index_scale_factor", RELOPT_TYPE_REAL,
3687		offsetof(BTOptions, vacuum_cleanup_index_scale_factor)},
3688		{"deduplicate_items", RELOPT_TYPE_BOOL,
3689		offsetof(BTOptions, deduplicate_items)}
3690	};
3691
3692	return (bytea *) build_reloptions(reloptions, validate,
3693									  RELOPT_KIND_BTREE,
3694									  sizeof(BTOptions),
3695									  tab, lengthof(tab));
3696}
3697
3698/*
3699 *	btproperty() -- Check boolean properties of indexes.
3700 *
3701 * This is optional, but handling AMPROP_RETURNABLE here saves opening the rel
3702 * to call btcanreturn.
3703 */
3704bool
3705btproperty(Oid index_oid, int attno,
3706		   IndexAMProperty prop, const char *propname,
3707		   bool *res, bool *isnull)
3708{
3709	switch (prop)
3710	{
3711		case AMPROP_RETURNABLE:
3712			/* answer only for columns, not AM or whole index */
3713			if (attno == 0)
3714				return false;
3715			/* otherwise, btree can always return data */
3716			*res = true;
3717			return true;
3718
3719		default:
3720			return false;		/* punt to generic code */
3721	}
3722}
3723
3724/*
3725 *	btbuildphasename() -- Return name of index build phase.
3726 */
3727char *
3728btbuildphasename(int64 phasenum)
3729{
3730	switch (phasenum)
3731	{
3732		case PROGRESS_CREATEIDX_SUBPHASE_INITIALIZE:
3733			return "initializing";
3734		case PROGRESS_BTREE_PHASE_INDEXBUILD_TABLESCAN:
3735			return "scanning table";
3736		case PROGRESS_BTREE_PHASE_PERFORMSORT_1:
3737			return "sorting live tuples";
3738		case PROGRESS_BTREE_PHASE_PERFORMSORT_2:
3739			return "sorting dead tuples";
3740		case PROGRESS_BTREE_PHASE_LEAF_LOAD:
3741			return "loading tuples in tree";
3742		default:
3743			return NULL;
3744	}
3745}
3746
3747/*
3748 *	_bt_truncate() -- create tuple without unneeded suffix attributes.
3749 *
3750 * Returns truncated pivot index tuple allocated in caller's memory context,
3751 * with key attributes copied from caller's firstright argument.  If rel is
3752 * an INCLUDE index, non-key attributes will definitely be truncated away,
3753 * since they're not part of the key space.  More aggressive suffix
3754 * truncation can take place when it's clear that the returned tuple does not
3755 * need one or more suffix key attributes.  We only need to keep firstright
3756 * attributes up to and including the first non-lastleft-equal attribute.
3757 * Caller's insertion scankey is used to compare the tuples; the scankey's
3758 * argument values are not considered here.
3759 *
3760 * Note that returned tuple's t_tid offset will hold the number of attributes
3761 * present, so the original item pointer offset is not represented.  Caller
3762 * should only change truncated tuple's downlink.  Note also that truncated
3763 * key attributes are treated as containing "minus infinity" values by
3764 * _bt_compare().
3765 *
3766 * In the worst case (when a heap TID must be appended to distinguish lastleft
3767 * from firstright), the size of the returned tuple is the size of firstright
3768 * plus the size of an additional MAXALIGN()'d item pointer.  This guarantee
3769 * is important, since callers need to stay under the 1/3 of a page
3770 * restriction on tuple size.  If this routine is ever taught to truncate
3771 * within an attribute/datum, it will need to avoid returning an enlarged
3772 * tuple to caller when truncation + TOAST compression ends up enlarging the
3773 * final datum.
3774 */
3775IndexTuple
3776_bt_truncate(Relation rel, IndexTuple lastleft, IndexTuple firstright,
3777			 BTScanInsert itup_key)
3778{
3779	TupleDesc	itupdesc = RelationGetDescr(rel);
3780	int16		nkeyatts = IndexRelationGetNumberOfKeyAttributes(rel);
3781	int			keepnatts;
3782	IndexTuple	pivot;
3783	IndexTuple	tidpivot;
3784	ItemPointer pivotheaptid;
3785	Size		newsize;
3786
3787	/*
3788	 * We should only ever truncate non-pivot tuples from leaf pages.  It's
3789	 * never okay to truncate when splitting an internal page.
3790	 */
3791	Assert(!BTreeTupleIsPivot(lastleft) && !BTreeTupleIsPivot(firstright));
3792
3793	/* Determine how many attributes must be kept in truncated tuple */
3794	keepnatts = _bt_keep_natts(rel, lastleft, firstright, itup_key);
3795
3796#ifdef DEBUG_NO_TRUNCATE
3797	/* Force truncation to be ineffective for testing purposes */
3798	keepnatts = nkeyatts + 1;
3799#endif
3800
3801	pivot = index_truncate_tuple(itupdesc, firstright,
3802								 Min(keepnatts, nkeyatts));
3803
3804	if (BTreeTupleIsPosting(pivot))
3805	{
3806		/*
3807		 * index_truncate_tuple() just returns a straight copy of firstright
3808		 * when it has no attributes to truncate.  When that happens, we may
3809		 * need to truncate away a posting list here instead.
3810		 */
3811		Assert(keepnatts == nkeyatts || keepnatts == nkeyatts + 1);
3812		Assert(IndexRelationGetNumberOfAttributes(rel) == nkeyatts);
3813		pivot->t_info &= ~INDEX_SIZE_MASK;
3814		pivot->t_info |= MAXALIGN(BTreeTupleGetPostingOffset(firstright));
3815	}
3816
3817	/*
3818	 * If there is a distinguishing key attribute within pivot tuple, we're
3819	 * done
3820	 */
3821	if (keepnatts <= nkeyatts)
3822	{
3823		BTreeTupleSetNAtts(pivot, keepnatts, false);
3824		return pivot;
3825	}
3826
3827	/*
3828	 * We have to store a heap TID in the new pivot tuple, since no non-TID
3829	 * key attribute value in firstright distinguishes the right side of the
3830	 * split from the left side.  nbtree conceptualizes this case as an
3831	 * inability to truncate away any key attributes, since heap TID is
3832	 * treated as just another key attribute (despite lacking a pg_attribute
3833	 * entry).
3834	 *
3835	 * Use enlarged space that holds a copy of pivot.  We need the extra space
3836	 * to store a heap TID at the end (using the special pivot tuple
3837	 * representation).  Note that the original pivot already has firstright's
3838	 * possible posting list/non-key attribute values removed at this point.
3839	 */
3840	newsize = MAXALIGN(IndexTupleSize(pivot)) + MAXALIGN(sizeof(ItemPointerData));
3841	tidpivot = palloc0(newsize);
3842	memcpy(tidpivot, pivot, MAXALIGN(IndexTupleSize(pivot)));
3843	/* Cannot leak memory here */
3844	pfree(pivot);
3845
3846	/*
3847	 * Store all of firstright's key attribute values plus a tiebreaker heap
3848	 * TID value in enlarged pivot tuple
3849	 */
3850	tidpivot->t_info &= ~INDEX_SIZE_MASK;
3851	tidpivot->t_info |= newsize;
3852	BTreeTupleSetNAtts(tidpivot, nkeyatts, true);
3853	pivotheaptid = BTreeTupleGetHeapTID(tidpivot);
3854
3855	/*
3856	 * Lehman & Yao use lastleft as the leaf high key in all cases, but don't
3857	 * consider suffix truncation.  It seems like a good idea to follow that
3858	 * example in cases where no truncation takes place -- use lastleft's heap
3859	 * TID.  (This is also the closest value to negative infinity that's
3860	 * legally usable.)
3861	 */
3862	ItemPointerCopy(BTreeTupleGetMaxHeapTID(lastleft), pivotheaptid);
3863
3864	/*
3865	 * We're done.  Assert() that heap TID invariants hold before returning.
3866	 *
3867	 * Lehman and Yao require that the downlink to the right page, which is to
3868	 * be inserted into the parent page in the second phase of a page split be
3869	 * a strict lower bound on items on the right page, and a non-strict upper
3870	 * bound for items on the left page.  Assert that heap TIDs follow these
3871	 * invariants, since a heap TID value is apparently needed as a
3872	 * tiebreaker.
3873	 */
3874#ifndef DEBUG_NO_TRUNCATE
3875	Assert(ItemPointerCompare(BTreeTupleGetMaxHeapTID(lastleft),
3876							  BTreeTupleGetHeapTID(firstright)) < 0);
3877	Assert(ItemPointerCompare(pivotheaptid,
3878							  BTreeTupleGetHeapTID(lastleft)) >= 0);
3879	Assert(ItemPointerCompare(pivotheaptid,
3880							  BTreeTupleGetHeapTID(firstright)) < 0);
3881#else
3882
3883	/*
3884	 * Those invariants aren't guaranteed to hold for lastleft + firstright
3885	 * heap TID attribute values when they're considered here only because
3886	 * DEBUG_NO_TRUNCATE is defined (a heap TID is probably not actually
3887	 * needed as a tiebreaker).  DEBUG_NO_TRUNCATE must therefore use a heap
3888	 * TID value that always works as a strict lower bound for items to the
3889	 * right.  In particular, it must avoid using firstright's leading key
3890	 * attribute values along with lastleft's heap TID value when lastleft's
3891	 * TID happens to be greater than firstright's TID.
3892	 */
3893	ItemPointerCopy(BTreeTupleGetHeapTID(firstright), pivotheaptid);
3894
3895	/*
3896	 * Pivot heap TID should never be fully equal to firstright.  Note that
3897	 * the pivot heap TID will still end up equal to lastleft's heap TID when
3898	 * that's the only usable value.
3899	 */
3900	ItemPointerSetOffsetNumber(pivotheaptid,
3901							   OffsetNumberPrev(ItemPointerGetOffsetNumber(pivotheaptid)));
3902	Assert(ItemPointerCompare(pivotheaptid,
3903							  BTreeTupleGetHeapTID(firstright)) < 0);
3904#endif
3905
3906	return tidpivot;
3907}
3908
3909/*
3910 * _bt_keep_natts - how many key attributes to keep when truncating.
3911 *
3912 * Caller provides two tuples that enclose a split point.  Caller's insertion
3913 * scankey is used to compare the tuples; the scankey's argument values are
3914 * not considered here.
3915 *
3916 * This can return a number of attributes that is one greater than the
3917 * number of key attributes for the index relation.  This indicates that the
3918 * caller must use a heap TID as a unique-ifier in new pivot tuple.
3919 */
3920static int
3921_bt_keep_natts(Relation rel, IndexTuple lastleft, IndexTuple firstright,
3922			   BTScanInsert itup_key)
3923{
3924	int			nkeyatts = IndexRelationGetNumberOfKeyAttributes(rel);
3925	TupleDesc	itupdesc = RelationGetDescr(rel);
3926	int			keepnatts;
3927	ScanKey		scankey;
3928
3929	/*
3930	 * _bt_compare() treats truncated key attributes as having the value minus
3931	 * infinity, which would break searches within !heapkeyspace indexes.  We
3932	 * must still truncate away non-key attribute values, though.
3933	 */
3934	if (!itup_key->heapkeyspace)
3935		return nkeyatts;
3936
3937	scankey = itup_key->scankeys;
3938	keepnatts = 1;
3939	for (int attnum = 1; attnum <= nkeyatts; attnum++, scankey++)
3940	{
3941		Datum		datum1,
3942					datum2;
3943		bool		isNull1,
3944					isNull2;
3945
3946		datum1 = index_getattr(lastleft, attnum, itupdesc, &isNull1);
3947		datum2 = index_getattr(firstright, attnum, itupdesc, &isNull2);
3948
3949		if (isNull1 != isNull2)
3950			break;
3951
3952		if (!isNull1 &&
3953			DatumGetInt32(FunctionCall2Coll(&scankey->sk_func,
3954											scankey->sk_collation,
3955											datum1,
3956											datum2)) != 0)
3957			break;
3958
3959		keepnatts++;
3960	}
3961
3962	/*
3963	 * Assert that _bt_keep_natts_fast() agrees with us in passing.  This is
3964	 * expected in an allequalimage index.
3965	 */
3966	Assert(!itup_key->allequalimage ||
3967		   keepnatts == _bt_keep_natts_fast(rel, lastleft, firstright));
3968
3969	return keepnatts;
3970}
3971
3972/*
3973 * _bt_keep_natts_fast - fast bitwise variant of _bt_keep_natts.
3974 *
3975 * This is exported so that a candidate split point can have its effect on
3976 * suffix truncation inexpensively evaluated ahead of time when finding a
3977 * split location.  A naive bitwise approach to datum comparisons is used to
3978 * save cycles.
3979 *
3980 * The approach taken here usually provides the same answer as _bt_keep_natts
3981 * will (for the same pair of tuples from a heapkeyspace index), since the
3982 * majority of btree opclasses can never indicate that two datums are equal
3983 * unless they're bitwise equal after detoasting.  When an index only has
3984 * "equal image" columns, routine is guaranteed to give the same result as
3985 * _bt_keep_natts would.
3986 *
3987 * Callers can rely on the fact that attributes considered equal here are
3988 * definitely also equal according to _bt_keep_natts, even when the index uses
3989 * an opclass or collation that is not "allequalimage"/deduplication-safe.
3990 * This weaker guarantee is good enough for nbtsplitloc.c caller, since false
3991 * negatives generally only have the effect of making leaf page splits use a
3992 * more balanced split point.
3993 */
3994int
3995_bt_keep_natts_fast(Relation rel, IndexTuple lastleft, IndexTuple firstright)
3996{
3997	TupleDesc	itupdesc = RelationGetDescr(rel);
3998	int			keysz = IndexRelationGetNumberOfKeyAttributes(rel);
3999	int			keepnatts;
4000
4001	keepnatts = 1;
4002	for (int attnum = 1; attnum <= keysz; attnum++)
4003	{
4004		Datum		datum1,
4005					datum2;
4006		bool		isNull1,
4007					isNull2;
4008		CompactAttribute *att;
4009
4010		datum1 = index_getattr(lastleft, attnum, itupdesc, &isNull1);
4011		datum2 = index_getattr(firstright, attnum, itupdesc, &isNull2);
4012		att = TupleDescCompactAttr(itupdesc, attnum - 1);
4013
4014		if (isNull1 != isNull2)
4015			break;
4016
4017		if (!isNull1 &&
4018			!datum_image_eq(datum1, datum2, att->attbyval, att->attlen))
4019			break;
4020
4021		keepnatts++;
4022	}
4023
4024	return keepnatts;
4025}
4026
4027/*
4028 *  _bt_check_natts() -- Verify tuple has expected number of attributes.
4029 *
4030 * Returns value indicating if the expected number of attributes were found
4031 * for a particular offset on page.  This can be used as a general purpose
4032 * sanity check.
4033 *
4034 * Testing a tuple directly with BTreeTupleGetNAtts() should generally be
4035 * preferred to calling here.  That's usually more convenient, and is always
4036 * more explicit.  Call here instead when offnum's tuple may be a negative
4037 * infinity tuple that uses the pre-v11 on-disk representation, or when a low
4038 * context check is appropriate.  This routine is as strict as possible about
4039 * what is expected on each version of btree.
4040 */
4041bool
4042_bt_check_natts(Relation rel, bool heapkeyspace, Page page, OffsetNumber offnum)
4043{
4044	int16		natts = IndexRelationGetNumberOfAttributes(rel);
4045	int16		nkeyatts = IndexRelationGetNumberOfKeyAttributes(rel);
4046	BTPageOpaque opaque = BTPageGetOpaque(page);
4047	IndexTuple	itup;
4048	int			tupnatts;
4049
4050	/*
4051	 * We cannot reliably test a deleted or half-dead page, since they have
4052	 * dummy high keys
4053	 */
4054	if (P_IGNORE(opaque))
4055		return true;
4056
4057	Assert(offnum >= FirstOffsetNumber &&
4058		   offnum <= PageGetMaxOffsetNumber(page));
4059
4060	itup = (IndexTuple) PageGetItem(page, PageGetItemId(page, offnum));
4061	tupnatts = BTreeTupleGetNAtts(itup, rel);
4062
4063	/* !heapkeyspace indexes do not support deduplication */
4064	if (!heapkeyspace && BTreeTupleIsPosting(itup))
4065		return false;
4066
4067	/* Posting list tuples should never have "pivot heap TID" bit set */
4068	if (BTreeTupleIsPosting(itup) &&
4069		(ItemPointerGetOffsetNumberNoCheck(&itup->t_tid) &
4070		 BT_PIVOT_HEAP_TID_ATTR) != 0)
4071		return false;
4072
4073	/* INCLUDE indexes do not support deduplication */
4074	if (natts != nkeyatts && BTreeTupleIsPosting(itup))
4075		return false;
4076
4077	if (P_ISLEAF(opaque))
4078	{
4079		if (offnum >= P_FIRSTDATAKEY(opaque))
4080		{
4081			/*
4082			 * Non-pivot tuple should never be explicitly marked as a pivot
4083			 * tuple
4084			 */
4085			if (BTreeTupleIsPivot(itup))
4086				return false;
4087
4088			/*
4089			 * Leaf tuples that are not the page high key (non-pivot tuples)
4090			 * should never be truncated.  (Note that tupnatts must have been
4091			 * inferred, even with a posting list tuple, because only pivot
4092			 * tuples store tupnatts directly.)
4093			 */
4094			return tupnatts == natts;
4095		}
4096		else
4097		{
4098			/*
4099			 * Rightmost page doesn't contain a page high key, so tuple was
4100			 * checked above as ordinary leaf tuple
4101			 */
4102			Assert(!P_RIGHTMOST(opaque));
4103
4104			/*
4105			 * !heapkeyspace high key tuple contains only key attributes. Note
4106			 * that tupnatts will only have been explicitly represented in
4107			 * !heapkeyspace indexes that happen to have non-key attributes.
4108			 */
4109			if (!heapkeyspace)
4110				return tupnatts == nkeyatts;
4111
4112			/* Use generic heapkeyspace pivot tuple handling */
4113		}
4114	}
4115	else						/* !P_ISLEAF(opaque) */
4116	{
4117		if (offnum == P_FIRSTDATAKEY(opaque))
4118		{
4119			/*
4120			 * The first tuple on any internal page (possibly the first after
4121			 * its high key) is its negative infinity tuple.  Negative
4122			 * infinity tuples are always truncated to zero attributes.  They
4123			 * are a particular kind of pivot tuple.
4124			 */
4125			if (heapkeyspace)
4126				return tupnatts == 0;
4127
4128			/*
4129			 * The number of attributes won't be explicitly represented if the
4130			 * negative infinity tuple was generated during a page split that
4131			 * occurred with a version of Postgres before v11.  There must be
4132			 * a problem when there is an explicit representation that is
4133			 * non-zero, or when there is no explicit representation and the
4134			 * tuple is evidently not a pre-pg_upgrade tuple.
4135			 *
4136			 * Prior to v11, downlinks always had P_HIKEY as their offset.
4137			 * Accept that as an alternative indication of a valid
4138			 * !heapkeyspace negative infinity tuple.
4139			 */
4140			return tupnatts == 0 ||
4141				ItemPointerGetOffsetNumber(&(itup->t_tid)) == P_HIKEY;
4142		}
4143		else
4144		{
4145			/*
4146			 * !heapkeyspace downlink tuple with separator key contains only
